title: Replace dual-write to Postgres and Kafka with a transactional outbox + relay
business_context: A fintech ledger service at a payments company processes 4,200 transfers/min. It writes the transfer to Postgres and then publishes a 'transfer.completed' event to Kafka for downstream reconciliation and notification services. During a 90s Kafka broker rebalance last quarter, 11,400 events were lost while the DB rows committed fine, so reconciliation flagged 11,400 phantom mismatches and the on-call team manually replayed for 6 hours.
constraints: No event may be lost even if Kafka is unavailable for up to 10 minutes
Consumers must tolerate duplicate events (at-least-once delivery)
Outbox relay must run across 6 service replicas without double-publishing
Transfer write path p99 must stay under 40ms; relay is async
Backward compatible: existing event schema 'transfer.completed' v2 must not change
Postgres is the source of truth; logical replication slots are not available on the managed tier

before_state.description: The handler commits the DB transaction then calls the Kafka producer in a separate step. If the process crashes or Kafka is down between the two, the event is silently dropped.

issues: DB commit and Kafka send are not atomic; crash between them loses the event
If producer.send throws after COMMIT, the row exists but no event is emitted
No retry or durability for the publish; broker rebalance drops the message

files.content_note: producer has no idempotent flag and acks default
issues: acks not set to 'all'; in-flight messages can be lost on broker failover
No idempotent producer config; relay would risk duplicates without consumer-side dedupe

reasoning: Naive fix: wrap the Kafka send in a retry loop after COMMIT. Rejected because a process crash (OOM, pod eviction) after COMMIT but before send leaves zero retries and a permanently lost event - retries only help when the process survives. Alternative considered: 2-phase commit / XA across Postgres and Kafka. Rejected because Kafka's transactional producer cannot enlist in a Postgres XA transaction and managed Postgres has no XA coordinator; it would also blow the 40ms p99. Chosen path is the transactional outbox: UI/API writes the transfer AND an outbox row in the SAME DB transaction (atomic). A separate relay process polls the outbox table (FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED so 6 replicas don't collide), publishes to Kafka with acks=all + idempotent producer, then marks the row published. Cross-layer: API handler -> single SQL transaction (transfers + outbox) -> relay poller -> kafkajs idempotent producer -> consumers dedupe on event_id -> config adds RELAY_BATCH and poll interval -> tests assert both rows commit together and relay publishes exactly the committed events.

after_state.description: Transfer and outbox event are written atomically. A relay polls unpublished outbox rows with SKIP LOCKED, publishes idempotently, and marks them published. Lost-event class eliminated.

files.purpose: Durable outbox table; partial index keeps the relay poll cheap by only scanning unpublished rows
Writes the business row and the event in one transaction so they can never diverge
Polls unpublished rows with SKIP LOCKED (safe across 6 replicas), publishes, then marks published in the same tx
Idempotent producer with acks=all (implied by idempotent) so broker failover does not drop in-flight messages

migration_strategy: Ship the outbox table migration and start writing outbox rows behind flag OUTBOX_WRITE=on while still doing the legacy post-commit send (dual emit)
Deploy the relay process disabled; verify outbox rows accumulate and published_at stays NULL in staging
Canary the relay on 1 replica with OUTBOX_RELAY=on for 5% of topics; consumers already dedupe so duplicates from dual emit are safe
Disable the legacy post-commit producer.send (OUTBOX_WRITE stays, legacy off); relay becomes sole publisher, ramp relay to all 6 replicas
Backfill: a one-off job re-emits any outbox rows older than the cutover with published_at NULL
Cleanup: remove legacy send code and the dual-emit flag after 7 days of zero reconciliation mismatches
testing_strategy: Integration test against a real Postgres (testcontainers) and an embedded Kafka. Fixture inserts 500 transfers concurrently across 4 simulated replicas. Assert output: every transfer row has exactly one outbox row (count match). Assert side-effects: relay publishes exactly 500 Kafka messages, each event_id unique, and all outbox rows end with published_at NOT NULL. Chaos test: kill the relay mid-batch (throw after producer.send, before UPDATE COMMIT) and restart - assert no transfer is double-counted by a consumer that dedupes on event-id header, and that the un-acked rows get re-published. Negative test: force producer.send to throw for 30s; assert outbox rows persist and drain once Kafka recovers.
what_breaks_naive_approach: In the naive dual-write, a pod eviction between COMMIT and producer.send leaves the transfer row durably committed with no event ever emitted; no retry runs because the process is gone, so reconciliation sees a row with no matching event - exactly the 11,400 phantom mismatches.

title: Make the Stripe webhook handler idempotent against duplicate deliveries
business_context: An e-commerce platform receives Stripe 'payment_intent.succeeded' webhooks to mark orders paid and decrement inventory. Stripe retries webhooks on any non-2xx and occasionally double-delivers. Last Black Friday, 320 orders were marked paid twice, double-decrementing inventory and triggering 320 duplicate fulfillment shipments that cost $48k in returns.
constraints: Stripe may deliver the same event up to 3 times; handler must be exactly-once in effect
Webhook endpoint must respond within 10s or Stripe marks it failed and retries
Runs on 8 autoscaled Lambda instances; no in-memory dedupe is reliable
Inventory decrement and order status update must be atomic
Must verify Stripe signature before any processing
Cannot change Stripe's at-least-once delivery semantics

before_state.description: The handler trusts each delivery as unique. Duplicate deliveries re-run the whole side-effecting flow.

issues: No dedupe on event.id; a redelivered event re-runs the decrement and fulfillment
Two queries are not in a transaction; partial application possible on crash
enqueueFulfillment runs every delivery, causing duplicate shipments

reasoning: Naive fix: keep a Set of processed event IDs in memory. Rejected because the handler runs on 8 stateless Lambdas - an in-memory set is not shared, so a duplicate routed to a different instance is not caught. Alternative: dedupe by orderId+status (skip if already paid). Rejected because it is not race-safe under concurrent duplicate deliveries (both read 'unpaid' then both write), and it can't distinguish a legitimate re-payment after refund. Chosen path: a processed_events table keyed on Stripe's event.id with a UNIQUE constraint, inserted inside the same DB transaction as the order/inventory mutations. The INSERT acts as the dedupe lock: a duplicate hits the unique violation and short-circuits with 200. Cross-layer: Stripe -> signature verify -> single tx (insert processed_event + update order + decrement inventory) -> on unique-violation return 200 without side-effects -> fulfillment enqueued only on first success -> config adds index -> tests assert one decrement under 3 concurrent duplicates.

after_state.description: Each Stripe event.id is recorded with a UNIQUE constraint inside the business transaction. Duplicates fail the insert and exit cleanly, guaranteeing exactly-once effects.

files.purpose: Idempotency ledger; PRIMARY KEY on event_id is the dedupe guarantee
Atomic dedupe-insert + order/inventory update + outbox fulfillment; duplicates exit at the unique violation

migration_strategy: Deploy the processed_webhook_events table and start inserting (best-effort, non-blocking) to backfill recent IDs behind IDEMPOTENT_WEBHOOK=shadow
Switch to enforcing mode on a canary stage receiving Stripe test-mode events; verify duplicates return 200 'duplicate'
Move fulfillment from direct enqueue to the outbox row, with the relay from shard-01 publishing it
Enable enforcing mode for 10% of production webhook traffic via a weighted route, monitor duplicate-skip counter
Ramp to 100%; keep the legacy direct-enqueue path dark for 48h for fast rollback
Remove legacy enqueueFulfillment call and the shadow flag after a clean 48h window
testing_strategy: Use a recorded real Stripe event fixture (signed test payload). Fire the same event.id 3 times concurrently with Promise.all against a real Postgres. Assert output: all three return 200. Assert side-effects: orders.status='paid' once, inventory.qty decremented by exactly 1, and exactly one outbox 'fulfillment.requested' row. Chaos test: crash the process (throw) after the order UPDATE but before COMMIT; on retry assert no partial state (status still unpaid, qty unchanged) and the event re-processes cleanly. Negative test: tampered signature must be rejected with 400 before any DB call.
what_breaks_naive_approach: The in-memory Set never sees duplicates that land on a different Lambda instance, and the non-transactional double UPDATE means a crash between the two leaves an order 'paid' with inventory not decremented - both failure modes produced the 320 double shipments.

title: Orchestrate hotel+flight+car booking with a compensating saga in Go
business_context: A travel SaaS books a trip as three independent provider calls (hotel, flight, car), each its own external API with its own commit. If the car booking fails after hotel and flight succeeded, the old code left the customer charged for a hotel and flight they may not want, generating ~140 support tickets/week and manual refunds averaging 3 days.
constraints: Each provider API is non-transactional and only offers book + cancel endpoints
Cancel endpoints are themselves at-least-once and may be called twice
A saga must survive process restarts (state persisted), not live only in memory
End-to-end booking p95 budget is 8s; compensation may run async
Provider 'cancel' has a 2h window after which it costs a fee; compensation must run promptly
Must not double-charge; partial bookings must be fully compensated or fully committed

before_state.description: A linear function calls the three providers in sequence. A failure on a later step throws and leaves earlier bookings live with no rollback.

issues: No compensation: a later failure leaves earlier bookings committed and charged
No persisted state; a crash mid-sequence loses track of what was booked
Cancel endpoints are never called, so customers are charged for orphan bookings

reasoning: Naive fix: add defer-based cancellation in the same function. Rejected because a process crash (not a returned error) skips the defers entirely, and an in-process defer can't compensate after a restart - the booking IDs are lost. Alternative: distributed 2PC across the three providers. Rejected because the providers expose only book/cancel REST endpoints with no prepare phase; 2PC is impossible without provider support. Chosen path: an orchestration saga with state persisted in Postgres after each step. Each forward step records the provider booking ID; on failure the orchestrator runs compensations in reverse for completed steps, using idempotent cancel calls keyed by a saga-scoped idempotency key. A recovery worker resumes sagas stuck in PENDING after a crash. Cross-layer: API -> create saga row -> step executor persists each booking_id -> on step error, compensator iterates completed steps in reverse calling cancel(idempotencyKey) -> recovery worker scans stale sagas -> config sets compensation deadline -> tests assert all-or-nothing.

after_state.description: A persisted saga records each step. On failure, completed steps are compensated in reverse with idempotent cancels. A recovery worker resumes interrupted sagas.

files.content:
CREATE TABLE booking_sagas (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY,
  state TEXT NOT NULL,
  step TEXT NOT NULL,
  payload JSONB NOT NULL,
  hotel_booking_id TEXT,
  flight_booking_id TEXT,
  car_booking_id TEXT,
  updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);
CREATE INDEX sagas_stale_idx ON booking_sagas (updated_at) WHERE state = 'PENDING';
files.purpose: Durable saga state so compensation and recovery survive restarts
Generic forward/compensate orchestration with state persisted after each forward step
Provider steps pass a saga-scoped idempotency key so retried book/cancel calls are deduped provider-side
Resumes sagas left PENDING by a crash, compensating from persisted booking IDs

migration_strategy: Introduce the saga table and orchestrator but keep BookTrip calling it in 'shadow' mode logging intended compensations without executing
Flip compensation execution on for internal test accounts (flag SAGA_COMPENSATE=on)
Canary 5% of real bookings through the saga path; alert on COMPENSATED rate
Enable the recovery worker on a single leader (advisory lock) and verify it resumes injected stale sagas
Ramp saga path to 100%; retire the linear BookTrip
Remove old linear function and shadow logging after 2 weeks of stable COMPENSATED metrics
testing_strategy: Fixture with three fake provider servers honoring idempotency keys. Happy path: assert all three booking IDs persisted and state=COMMITTED. Failure path: make carAPI.Book return 500; assert hotel and flight Cancel were each called exactly once (idempotency key recorded), saga state=COMPENSATED, and customer charge net zero. Chaos test: kill the process after flight is booked but before car; restart and run RecoverStale; assert the recovery compensates hotel and flight idempotently (calling Cancel twice via duplicate recovery does not error or double-fee). Side-effect assert: provider fake records exactly one effective cancel per booking despite duplicate cancel calls.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A crash (SIGKILL/OOM) between flight and car booking skips all defers and loses the in-memory hotel/flight IDs, so nothing ever cancels them - the customer stays charged for two orphan bookings, which is the recurring support-ticket source.

title: Fix lost-update race on wallet balance with optimistic concurrency
business_context: A gaming platform credits and debits a player's coin wallet from multiple services (purchases, rewards, tournament payouts). Under concurrent updates the read-modify-write pattern lost updates: during a tournament, 2,300 payout credits silently vanished because concurrent debits overwrote them, generating a wave of 'where are my coins' tickets and a $9k goodwill credit bill.
constraints: Up to 12 concurrent writers per wallet during tournament payouts
Balance must never go negative; debits must be rejected, not clamped
No table locks held across HTTP calls (other services share the DB)
Reads for display can be slightly stale; writes must be strictly serializable per wallet
Postgres only; must work on the managed read-replica-fronted setup
Backward compatible API: response shape unchanged

before_state.description: Balance is read into the app, modified, and written back. Concurrent writers read the same starting balance and clobber each other.

issues: Read-modify-write race: two concurrent debits both read the same balance and one write is lost
No version check; last write wins and silently drops the other update
Insufficient check uses stale in-app value, can allow overdraft under concurrency

reasoning: Naive fix: SELECT ... FOR UPDATE to lock the row. Rejected as the primary approach because the team also needs to detect cross-request conflicts and the constraint forbids holding locks across the HTTP boundary; FOR UPDATE is fine within one short tx but doesn't surface conflicts to callers and serializes all 12 writers behind one lock, hurting tournament throughput. Alternative: SERIALIZABLE isolation. Rejected because it would force broad app-wide retry handling for serialization failures on unrelated queries sharing the connection. Chosen path: optimistic concurrency with a version column and a conditional UPDATE that both checks balance and bumps version atomically in a single statement; if 0 rows update, retry with bounded backoff. The single-statement UPDATE ... WHERE balance >= amount makes the overdraft check and the write atomic at the DB. Cross-layer: API -> conditional UPDATE (atomic check+decrement+version bump) -> 0 rows => retry up to N -> ledger row inserted in same tx -> config sets retry count -> tests assert no lost updates under 12 writers.

after_state.description: Debit is a single atomic conditional UPDATE that checks balance and bumps version. Conflicts (0 rows affected) trigger bounded retry. A ledger row records every movement.

files.purpose: Version column for optimistic concurrency, CHECK to make negative balance impossible at the DB, ledger for audit
Atomic check-and-decrement; the WHERE balance >= amount makes overdraft impossible without app-side races
Credits are commutative increments at the DB level so concurrent payouts never lose each other

migration_strategy: Add version column (default 0) and CHECK constraint in a non-blocking migration; existing reads unaffected
Deploy new debit/credit behind WALLET_ATOMIC=on for internal QA wallets only
Replay a captured tournament payout burst against a staging copy; verify ledger sum equals balance
Canary 10% of wallets to the atomic path; monitor retry-exhausted error rate
Ramp to 100%; remove old read-modify-write code path
Add a nightly reconciliation comparing SUM(ledger.delta) to balance; alert on drift, then drop the legacy flag
testing_strategy: Fixture seeds a wallet at 1000 coins. Fire 12 concurrent debits of 100 and 5 concurrent credits of 50 via Promise.all on a real Postgres. Assert output: final balance equals 1000 - 12*100 + 5*50 with zero lost updates, and ledger row count equals 17. Side-effect assert: SUM(wallet_ledger.delta) + initial == final balance. Negative test: 12 concurrent debits of 200 against balance 1000 - assert exactly 5 succeed, 7 throw InsufficientFundsError, balance never negative, CHECK never violated. Chaos test: inject a serialization-style retry storm by forcing rowCount=0 on first 3 attempts; assert it converges within the 5-retry budget.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Two concurrent debits both SELECT balance=1000, each compute 900, and one UPDATE overwrites the other - one debit is silently lost. With 12 writers this drops most concurrent updates, exactly how the 2,300 tournament credits vanished.

title: Add idempotency keys to the POST /payments API to stop double charges on retry
business_context: A B2B SaaS billing API lets customers charge their end-users. Mobile clients on flaky networks retry POST /payments when a response times out, but the original request often succeeded server-side, producing duplicate charges. 1,900 duplicate charges in one month triggered chargebacks and a near-breach of the payment processor's dispute threshold.
constraints: Clients may retry the same logical payment with the same Idempotency-Key header
Two concurrent requests with the same key must result in exactly one charge
Stored idempotency response must be replayable for 24h
Runs on 4 API replicas behind a load balancer; no sticky sessions
Must reject a reused key whose request body differs (key reuse attack)
Charge call to processor is non-idempotent and slow (700ms p50)

before_state.description: Every POST creates a new charge. No dedupe on the client-supplied key; concurrent retries both hit the processor.

issues: Ignores Idempotency-Key entirely; every retry creates a new processor charge
Charge happens before the DB write, so a duplicate is charged even if the row insert is the dedupe point
No concurrency guard; two simultaneous retries both call the processor

reasoning: Naive fix: check if a payment with req.id exists, skip if so. Rejected because two concurrent retries both SELECT 'not found' before either inserts (TOCTOU), so both call the processor. Alternative: a Redis SETNX lock on the key. Rejected as sole mechanism because if the process dies holding the lock the key is stuck until TTL and the stored response is lost; also Redis and Postgres can diverge. Chosen path: an idempotency_keys table with UNIQUE(key) inserted in a 'pending' state at request start within a transaction; the unique insert is the race-safe gate. The first request claims the key, calls the processor, then stores the final response on the same row. A concurrent second request loses the unique insert, then polls/reads the stored response (or returns 409 if still pending and replays once complete). Body-hash mismatch on key reuse returns 422. Cross-layer: client header -> claim key via unique insert -> processor charge -> persist response on key row -> concurrent dup reads stored response -> config sets 24h TTL sweep -> tests assert single charge under concurrent retries.

after_state.description: A unique idempotency_keys row gates each logical payment. First caller charges and stores the response; duplicates replay the stored response. Body mismatch is rejected.

files.purpose: Idempotency ledger keyed by client header; stores hash for body-mismatch detection and cached response for replay
ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING claims the key race-safely; only the winner charges, then caches the response
Duplicate requests replay the stored response, detect body mismatch, and wait briefly for an in-flight owner

migration_strategy: Deploy the idempotency_keys table; require the Idempotency-Key header but treat missing keys as before (flag IDEM_REQUIRED=off)
Enable claim/replay logic for clients sending the header; legacy header-less requests unchanged
Roll the mobile client update that always sends a UUID Idempotency-Key per logical payment
Flip IDEM_REQUIRED=on once >95% of traffic carries the header; header-less requests get 400
Add a sweeper deleting idempotency rows older than 24h
Remove the legacy charge-then-insert code path after a week of zero duplicate-charge reports
testing_strategy: Fixture stubs the processor to count charge calls and adds 700ms latency. Fire two concurrent requests with the same key and identical body against 2 app instances sharing Postgres. Assert output: both responses identical (same processorRef). Side-effect assert: processor.charge called exactly once, exactly one payments row, one idempotency_keys row status='done'. Negative test: reuse the same key with a different amount -> 422. Chaos test: kill the owning process after charge but before the UPDATE-to-done; on client retry assert the stored payments row lets reconciliation recover and no second charge occurs (processor still called once total because the payment row carries the processor_ref).
what_breaks_naive_approach: The existence-check has a TOCTOU window: two concurrent retries both read 'no row', both call the non-idempotent processor, and both insert - producing the duplicate charge and chargeback that the existence check was meant to prevent.

title: Make Kafka order-events consumer exactly-once with offset+DB in one transaction
business_context: A logistics platform consumes 'shipment.scanned' events from Kafka to update shipment status in Postgres. The consumer commits offsets after processing, but a crash between the DB write and the offset commit reprocesses events, double-incrementing scan counts. A warehouse outage replayed 84,000 events, inflating scan counts and breaking SLA dashboards for two days.
constraints: Consumer runs in a group of 6 partitions across 3 pods; rebalances happen on deploy
Status updates must be applied exactly once per event despite at-least-once delivery
Cannot use Kafka transactions across the external Postgres (no XA)
Throughput must stay above 5k events/sec
Event payload carries a monotonically increasing scan sequence per shipment
Backward compatible with existing downstream consumers of the status table

before_state.description: The consumer processes the message, writes to DB, then commits the offset. The two are independent, so a crash reprocesses.

issues: DB write and offset commit are separate; crash between them reprocesses the event
scan_count += 1 is not idempotent on reprocessing
No dedupe on the per-shipment scan sequence; replays double-count

reasoning: Naive fix: commit the offset before the DB write. Rejected because then a crash after commit but before the write loses the update entirely (at-most-once). Alternative: enable Kafka exactly-once with transactional producer/consumer. Rejected because the side effect lands in external Postgres which can't enlist in Kafka's transaction; EOS only covers Kafka->Kafka. Chosen path: store the consumed offset in Postgres in the SAME transaction as the status update, and make the update idempotent by tracking last_applied_seq per shipment so replayed events are no-ops. On startup, seek each partition to the offset stored in Postgres, making the DB the single source of progress. Cross-layer: Kafka -> seek to DB-stored offset on assign -> single tx (update status if seq>last + upsert offset) -> idempotent on replay -> config disables auto-commit -> tests assert replay does not double-count.

after_state.description: Offset and status update commit atomically in Postgres; the consumer seeks to the stored offset on partition assignment; per-shipment sequence makes replays idempotent.

files.purpose: Stores consumer progress in Postgres so offset and side-effect commit atomically; last_applied_seq makes updates idempotent
Atomic status update + offset persist in one tx; seq guard makes replays no-ops
On rebalance, resumes each partition from the Postgres-stored offset, making the DB the source of progress truth

migration_strategy: Add consumer_offsets table and last_applied_seq column; keep Kafka auto-commit on (dual tracking) behind EOS_CONSUMER=shadow
Verify in staging that DB-stored offsets track Kafka offsets within a few messages
Switch one pod to autoCommit=false and seek-from-DB; monitor for gaps or lag
Roll all pods to DB-managed offsets; disable Kafka auto-commit fully
Replay a recorded burst in staging to confirm zero double-counts
Remove shadow dual-tracking and old commit code after a clean week
testing_strategy: Embedded Kafka + real Postgres. Fixture publishes 1,000 shipment.scanned events with increasing seq. Assert output: every shipment's scan_count equals its event count. Side-effect assert: consumer_offsets.next_offset equals the high-water mark per partition. Chaos test: kill the consumer pod mid-batch (after COMMIT of message N, before N+1) then restart; assert it seeks to N+1 from Postgres and no shipment is double-counted. Replay test: re-deliver the entire 1,000-event batch; assert scan_count unchanged because seq <= last_applied_seq guards every row.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A crash after the DB UPDATE but before commitOffsets means Kafka redelivers the message; the non-idempotent scan_count += 1 runs again, double-counting - precisely the 84k-event replay inflation.

title: Convert MongoDB multi-doc order placement to a transaction with retryable writes
business_context: A marketplace stores orders, order_items, and a seller_balances doc in MongoDB. Order placement writes them sequentially without a transaction; a mid-sequence failure left orphaned order_items and credited seller balances for orders that were never finalized. Finance found $73k of phantom seller credits over a quarter.
constraints: MongoDB replica set 4.4+ with multi-document transactions available
Writes must be all-or-nothing across 3 collections
Transient transaction errors (write conflicts) must be retried, not surfaced
Order placement p95 must stay under 250ms
Seller balance increments must be exactly-once per order
Existing read paths must not require schema changes

before_state.description: Three sequential inserts/updates with no session. A failure between them leaves partial state.

issues: No transaction/session; failure after orders insert leaves items missing or balance uncredited
Seller balance can be credited without a completed order (no atomicity)
Retried calls re-credit the balance (not idempotent)

reasoning: Naive fix: wrap the three writes in try/catch and manually delete on failure. Rejected because manual compensation is racy and a crash skips the catch, leaving the exact orphan state. Alternative: keep separate writes but add a nightly reconciliation to fix orphans. Rejected because it leaves a multi-hour window of incorrect seller balances driving payouts. Chosen path: a MongoDB multi-document transaction via a client session with majority write concern, wrapped in withTransaction (which auto-retries TransientTransactionError and UnknownTransactionCommitResult). Idempotency is enforced by a unique index on orders._id so a retried placement that already committed is rejected and not re-credited. Cross-layer: API -> startSession -> withTransaction(insert order + items + $inc balance) -> auto-retry transient conflicts -> unique _id blocks double-credit -> config sets majority concern -> tests assert all-or-nothing.

after_state.description: All three writes run in one ACID transaction with majority concern and automatic retry of transient errors; unique order id prevents double-credit.

files.purpose: Single ACID transaction with majority concern; withTransaction auto-retries transient write conflicts
Unique idempotencyKey index makes a retried placement fail fast instead of re-crediting the seller
Maps duplicate-key (11000) to an idempotent replay response instead of a second order/credit

migration_strategy: Create the unique idempotencyKey index (sparse) without backfilling; new placements include the key
Deploy transactional placeOrder behind ORDER_TX=on for 5% of sellers
Run the reconciliation job once to clean historical orphan items and phantom credits before ramping
Ramp ORDER_TX to 100%; monitor transaction retry counts and commit latency p95
Require Idempotency-Key from clients; reject header-less placements after 95% adoption
Remove the non-transactional code path and the reconciliation cron after two clean weeks
testing_strategy: Integration test against a real replica set (mongodb-memory-server with replset). Fixture places an order with 5 items. Assert output: orderId returned, 1 order doc, 5 item docs, balance.pending incremented by total. Chaos test: force insertMany(items) to throw inside the transaction; assert the orders doc and the balance $inc are both rolled back (collections unchanged). Idempotency test: replay the same idempotencyKey concurrently x3; assert exactly one order, one set of items, one balance increment, and the duplicates return {replay:true}. Side-effect assert: seller_balances.pending equals sum of distinct order totals only.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Without a session, a failure after the order_items insert but before the balance update (or a retry of the whole call) credits the seller for an order that may not exist or double-credits on retry - the source of the $73k phantom credits.

title: Eliminate cache-vs-DB staleness with transactional cache invalidation via outbox
business_context: A social platform caches user profile docs in Redis with a 10-minute TTL. Profile edits update Postgres then delete the Redis key, but the delete sometimes runs before the DB commit is visible to replicas, so a concurrent read repopulates the cache with stale data that then persists for the full TTL. Users reported edits 'reverting' for up to 10 minutes; 4,100 support reports in a month.
constraints: Reads come from 200k req/min and must be served from cache; cache-aside pattern stays
DB uses a primary + 2 read replicas with up to 80ms replication lag
Invalidation must not be lost if Redis is briefly unavailable
No distributed lock per profile read (too hot)
Edits are low volume (~50/sec) but must be immediately consistent on next read
Backward compatible cache key scheme

before_state.description: Edit updates the primary then deletes the cache key. A concurrent read against a lagging replica repopulates stale data after the delete.

issues: del happens then a concurrent read repopulates from a stale replica, re-caching old data
Read uses a replica that lags the write, so even after invalidation it can cache stale rows
If redis.del fails, stale data lives for the full 10-min TTL with no retry

reasoning: Naive fix: delete the cache key after the update with a retry. Rejected because the real bug is the read-repopulation race against a lagging replica, not a failed delete - retrying del doesn't stop a concurrent reader from re-caching stale replica data. Alternative: write-through cache (update Redis inside the same path). Rejected because Redis and Postgres updates aren't atomic and a crash between them desyncs them. Chosen path: version-stamped cache values plus delayed double-invalidation driven by an outbox. The profile row carries a monotonic version; cache stores {version, data}. getProfile reads from the primary for the authoritative version when a cache miss could be a post-edit race, and writes are followed by an outbox 'profile.invalidate' event whose relay deletes the key twice (immediately and after replica-lag delay), so any stale repopulation is wiped. A reader only caches a value if its version >= the version recorded in a short-lived 'min-version' marker. Cross-layer: edit -> UPDATE + bump version + outbox invalidate (one tx) -> relay deletes key now and after 200ms -> reader compares version and refuses to cache stale -> config sets lag delay -> tests assert no stale cache survives.

after_state.description: Profiles carry a version. Edits write an outbox invalidate event atomically; the relay double-deletes the key past replication lag. Readers refuse to cache a version older than the recorded minimum.

files.content:
ALTER TABLE profiles ADD COLUMN version BIGINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0;
-- outbox_events reused from the platform outbox
files.purpose: Monotonic version per profile to detect stale reads at cache-write time
Bumps version, records an invalidate outbox event atomically, and sets a short-lived min-version floor
Double-deletes the cache key immediately and after replication-lag delay to kill stale repopulation
Refuses to cache a replica read whose version is below the recent edit floor, closing the repopulation race

migration_strategy: Add version column (default 0); deploy version-aware get/set behind CACHE_V2=shadow logging would-be skips
Enable min-version floor writes on edit without changing read caching yet
Turn on version-gated caching in get for 10% of profiles; monitor stale-cache reports
Enable the outbox-driven double-invalidation relay; ramp reads to 100%
Tune the lag delay from replication-lag metrics (p99 lag + margin)
Remove the legacy del-after-update code and CACHE_V2 flag after stale reports hit zero
testing_strategy: Fixture with real Redis and a Postgres primary+replica harness that injects 80ms lag. Edit a profile, then immediately issue 50 concurrent reads routed to the lagging replica. Assert output: no read returns the pre-edit bio after the invalidate event is processed. Side-effect assert: the cache key is either absent or holds the new version; never the old version. Chaos test: drop Redis for 1s during the edit; assert the outbox event persists and the relay deletes the key on recovery, and that the min-version floor prevents caching stale data during the outage. Negative test: a read with version below the floor must not write the cache key.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Right after del, a concurrent reader hitting a replica 80ms behind reads the old row and re-caches it; the stale value then survives the full 10-minute TTL - exactly the 'edits reverting' reports.

title: Fix double-spend in inventory reservation with a conditional reserve+commit flow
business_context: An e-commerce flash-sale system reserves stock at add-to-cart and commits it at checkout. The old reserve just decremented a counter, but abandoned carts never released stock and concurrent reservations oversold limited SKUs. A 500-unit sneaker drop oversold by 137 units, forcing cancellations and brand damage.
constraints: Up to 8,000 concurrent reserve attempts in the first 5s of a drop
Reservations must auto-expire after 15 minutes if not committed
Must never oversell; available = on_hand - active_reservations >= 0 always
Reserve and commit run on different requests and possibly different pods
Redis is the hot path for reservations; Postgres is source of truth
Idempotent commit: a retried checkout must not consume stock twice

before_state.description: Reserve decrements a Redis counter; commit decrements Postgres. No expiry, no atomic guard, no idempotency.

issues: decrby then check is not atomic with the compensating incrby; concurrent reserves oversell in the gap
No reservation expiry; abandoned carts hold stock forever
commit is not idempotent; a retried checkout double-decrements on_hand

reasoning: Naive fix: use Redis WATCH/MULTI around the decrement. Rejected because WATCH retries thrash under 8,000 concurrent writers on a single hot key and still don't give expiry or idempotent commit. Alternative: a Lua script that decrements only if enough stock, plus per-reservation TTL keys. The Lua atomic check-and-decrement is the right hot-path guard; we keep it but add a reservations hash with per-reservation expiry and a reaper, plus an idempotent commit keyed by reservationId. Rejected pure-Postgres approach (SELECT FOR UPDATE per reserve) because it can't sustain 8k/sec on one row. Chosen path: a Lua script performs available-check + decrement + record reservation with expiry atomically; a reaper releases expired reservations back to available; commit is idempotent via a committed-set membership check, then reflected to Postgres via outbox. Cross-layer: reserve -> Lua atomic guard in Redis -> reservation TTL -> reaper releases expired -> commit checks committed-set then outbox to Postgres -> config sets TTL/reaper interval -> tests assert never oversell under 8k concurrency.

after_state.description: A Lua script atomically checks availability, decrements, and records a reservation with expiry. A reaper releases expired reservations. Commit is idempotent and reflected to Postgres via outbox.

files.content:
-- KEYS[1]=stock:sku KEYS[2]=resv:sku  ARGV[1]=qty ARGV[2]=reservationId ARGV[3]=expireAtMs
local avail = tonumber(redis.call('GET', KEYS[1]) or '0')
local qty = tonumber(ARGV[1])
if avail < qty then return -1 end
redis.call('DECRBY', KEYS[1], qty)
redis.call('HSET', KEYS[2], ARGV[2], qty)
redis.call('ZADD', 'resv:expiry', ARGV[3], KEYS[2] .. '|' .. ARGV[2])
return avail - qty
files.purpose: Atomic availability-check + decrement + reservation record; impossible to oversell because the check and decrement are one Redis operation
Calls the atomic Lua guard; never oversells even under 8k concurrent callers
Releases stock from reservations that expired without checkout, fixing the abandoned-cart leak
Idempotent commit via committed-set membership; reflects consumption to Postgres through the outbox

migration_strategy: Load the Lua script and dual-run: legacy decrby plus new reserve path comparing results behind RESERVE_V2=shadow
Seed Redis stock counters from Postgres on_hand for all active SKUs
Enable the reaper on a single leader; verify expired reservations release correctly in staging load test
Canary RESERVE_V2 for one low-volume SKU drop, then ramp to all drops
Switch commit to idempotent + outbox; relay reflects to Postgres on_hand
Remove legacy decrby/commit and the shadow comparison after a clean drop event
testing_strategy: Load test fixture fires 8,000 concurrent reserves for a SKU with on_hand=500 against real Redis. Assert output: exactly 500 succeed, 7,500 get OutOfStockError, Redis counter never below 0. Side-effect assert: SUM of active reservation quantities == 500. Reaper test: create 100 reservations, advance time past TTL, run reaper; assert all 100 release and available returns to original. Idempotency/chaos test: call commit for the same reservationId 3 times concurrently; assert exactly one outbox 'inventory.committed' row and on_hand decremented once. Negative test: commit an expired (reaped) reservationId; assert it does not over-consume because the reservation no longer holds stock.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Between decrby returning a value and the compensating incrby, other concurrent reservers already saw the over-decremented counter as negative-but-they-decremented-too, so multiple oversell simultaneously; with 8k writers the 137-unit oversell is the result.

title: Replace at-least-once email side-effect with outbox + idempotent send to stop duplicate emails
business_context: A healthcare appointment system sends reminder emails when an appointment is confirmed. The confirm handler writes to Postgres then calls SendGrid; on transient SendGrid 5xx it retries the whole handler, re-confirming and re-emailing. Patients received up to 5 duplicate reminders; 1 patient complaint escalated to a compliance review because a cancelled appointment still emailed a reminder.
constraints: Email send must be exactly-once in effect per appointment confirmation
SendGrid is non-transactional and occasionally returns 5xx that later succeeds
PHI handling: no email may be sent for a cancelled/changed appointment
Confirm handler runs on 5 replicas; retries can land anywhere
Reminder must send within 2 minutes of confirmation
Backward compatible with the existing appointments schema

before_state.description: Confirm writes the row then sends email inline. Handler retries re-run both, and a later cancellation can't recall an already-sent email.

issues: Email send is inline and retried with the whole handler; transient 5xx causes duplicate sends
No dedupe per appointment; multiple confirms re-email
No state gate; a cancelled appointment between confirm and send still emails

reasoning: Naive fix: catch SendGrid 5xx and retry only the send. Rejected because a process crash mid-retry loses the send entirely, and a duplicate confirm still double-emails. Alternative: a 'sent' boolean flag set after send. Rejected because set-after-send is not atomic with the send (crash between leaves flag false -> resend) and concurrent confirms both read flag=false. Chosen path: write an outbox 'reminder.requested' row atomically with the confirm, and a dispatcher that sends via SendGrid using SendGrid's own idempotency-key plus a sent-state transition guarded by a unique constraint, and re-checks appointment status == confirmed at send time so cancellations suppress the email. Cross-layer: confirm -> UPDATE + outbox (one tx) -> dispatcher reads pending reminder -> re-validates status==confirmed -> SendGrid with idempotency key -> mark sent (unique guard) -> config sets dispatch interval -> tests assert exactly-one email and no email for cancelled.

after_state.description: Confirm enqueues a reminder via outbox atomically. A dispatcher re-validates status and sends with an idempotency key, transitioning the reminder to 'sent' under a unique guard.

files.purpose: Reminder ledger; unique per appointment prevents duplicate reminders, idempotency_key passed to SendGrid
Atomically confirms and enqueues at most one reminder; ON CONFLICT makes repeated confirms a no-op
Sends with SendGrid idempotency key, re-validates status so cancellations suppress, marks sent under a guarded update

migration_strategy: Add reminders table; keep inline send live but also write the reminder row behind REMINDER_OUTBOX=shadow
Verify reminder rows are created 1:1 with confirms in staging
Enable the dispatcher to send; disable the inline send (flag flip) for a canary clinic
Confirm zero duplicate emails for the canary over 48h, then ramp to all clinics
Add an alert on reminders stuck 'pending' > 5 minutes (dispatcher health)
Remove inline send and shadow flag after a clean week with compliance sign-off
testing_strategy: Fixture with a fake SendGrid recording sends by idempotency key. Confirm an appointment, run the dispatcher; assert exactly one email with the reminder template. Retry test: call confirm 4 times then dispatch; assert one reminder row, one email. Chaos test: make SendGrid return 5xx on first dispatch, succeed on second; assert idempotency key dedupes so the patient still gets exactly one email and the row ends 'sent'. PHI/negative test: confirm then cancel the appointment before dispatch; assert the reminder is 'suppressed' and zero emails sent. Side-effect assert: no reminder row ever reaches both 'sent' and 'suppressed'.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A transient SendGrid 5xx retries the whole confirm handler, re-running the UPDATE and re-sending the email; and because the email already fired, a subsequent cancellation can't recall it - producing both the duplicate reminders and the cancelled-appointment email that triggered compliance review.

title: Make a Django bank transfer atomic with select_for_update and row-level locking
business_context: A neobank's Django service moves money between accounts. The transfer view debits one account and credits another in two separate saves. Under concurrent transfers touching the same account, balances drifted and one customer overdrew by $2,400 because two transfers read the same starting balance. Finance halted automated transfers for a week.
constraints: Up to 30 concurrent transfers can touch a single hot account (payroll runs)
Balance must never go negative; overdraft must raise, not clamp
Deadlocks must be avoided when two transfers touch the same pair of accounts
Transfers must be idempotent on client retry (network double-submit)
Postgres backend; runs on 4 gunicorn workers
Existing serializer/response contract unchanged

before_state.description: Two account loads and saves with no locking and no transaction boundary; concurrent transfers lose updates and can overdraw.

issues: No transaction; a crash between the two saves leaves money debited but not credited
No row locking; concurrent transfers read stale balances and overdraw
No idempotency; a retried POST runs the transfer twice

reasoning: Naive fix: wrap the two saves in transaction.atomic(). Rejected as insufficient because atomic() alone doesn't prevent the lost-update race - two transactions still read the same balance before either writes. Alternative: F() expressions for in-place updates (balance = F('balance') - amount). This fixes lost updates for the arithmetic but can't enforce the non-negative check atomically and doesn't order locks to avoid deadlock. Chosen path: transaction.atomic() + select_for_update() on both rows, locking in a deterministic order (sorted by id) to avoid deadlock, with the overdraft check performed after acquiring the lock, plus an idempotency table keyed on a client transfer reference. Cross-layer: API -> atomic tx -> lock both rows ordered by id -> re-check balance under lock -> apply -> idempotency row blocks retry -> config sets lock timeout -> tests assert no overdraft under 30 writers and no deadlock.

after_state.description: Transfer runs in one atomic transaction, locking both account rows in id order, re-checking balance under the lock, and recording an idempotency key to dedupe retries.

files.purpose: Atomic transfer with ordered row locks (no deadlock), under-lock balance check (no overdraft), and idempotency

files.content:
from django.db import models

class TransferKey(models.Model):
    key = models.CharField(max_length=64, unique=True)
    src_id = models.UUIDField()
    dst_id = models.UUIDField()
    amount = models.DecimalField(max_digits=18, decimal_places=2)
    created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)
files.purpose: Idempotency ledger; unique key makes a retried transfer a no-op replay
DB-level guard making a negative balance impossible even if app logic regresses

migration_strategy: Add TransferKey model and the CHECK constraint (validate existing rows are non-negative first)
Deploy locked transfer behind TRANSFER_LOCK=on for internal test accounts
Require Idempotency-Key header from clients; reject header-less after rollout
Canary 10% of transfers; monitor deadlock and lock-timeout metrics
Ramp to 100%; remove the unlocked code path
Add a reconciliation comparing sum of debits/credits per account daily, then drop the flag
testing_strategy: Use Django TransactionTestCase with real Postgres. Fixture: account A=1000, B=0. Fire 30 concurrent transfers of 100 A->B using threads. Assert output: A ends at -? no: assert A=1000-30*100 only if sufficient; cap so that exactly 10 succeed and 20 raise insufficient, A never below 0. Side-effect assert: A.balance + B.balance conserved (sum constant). Deadlock test: 50 concurrent pairs transferring A<->B in both directions; assert zero deadlock errors due to id-ordered locking. Idempotency test: same Idempotency-Key submitted twice concurrently; assert exactly one transfer applied, second returns replay. Chaos: kill a worker mid-atomic; assert no partial debit/credit persists.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Two concurrent transfers both read src.balance=1000, both pass the check, both save 900 - one debit is lost and the account can be overdrawn, exactly the $2,400 overdraft. transaction.atomic() alone wouldn't fix it because both transactions still read the pre-write balance.

title: Add a dead-letter + retry pipeline so failed saga steps don't silently drop
business_context: A subscription billing platform runs a provisioning saga (create account -> allocate license -> send welcome). When the license-allocation provider had a 20-minute outage, 612 saga executions threw and were lost with no retry, leaving customers who paid but never got provisioned. Support manually reprovisioned over 3 days.
constraints: Failed steps must be retried with exponential backoff, capped at 6 attempts
Permanently failed sagas must land in a dead-letter queue for human action, never vanish
Retries must be idempotent (no duplicate provisioning)
Saga state persisted in Postgres; workers are 4 replicas
Backoff must not hammer a downed provider (jittered)
A replayed DLQ item must resume from the failed step, not from the start

before_state.description: Saga steps run once; a thrown step bubbles up and the execution is dropped with only a log line.

issues: No retry; a transient provider error permanently drops the saga
No dead-letter; failures are only logged, never recoverable
No resume point; even a manual replay restarts from step 0, risking duplicate provisioning

reasoning: Naive fix: wrap step.execute in a try/catch with a fixed retry loop. Rejected because in-process retries die with the process during a 20-minute outage and a tight loop hammers the provider. Alternative: push failures to a Kafka DLQ topic only. Rejected as sole mechanism because without persisted saga progress, a DLQ replay can't resume from the failed step and re-runs idempotency-unsafe earlier steps. Chosen path: persist saga step progress in Postgres; on step failure schedule a retry row with jittered exponential backoff and an attempt counter; a scheduler picks due retries (FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED); after 6 attempts move the saga to a dead_letter table with the failed step recorded so a human/replay resumes from exactly that step using idempotency keys. Cross-layer: worker -> execute step -> on error insert retry row with next_run + attempt -> scheduler claims due retries -> resume from current_step idempotently -> after cap -> dead_letter -> config sets backoff base/cap -> tests assert no drop and resume-from-step.

after_state.description: Saga progress is persisted. Failed steps are retried with jittered backoff up to a cap; exhausted sagas go to a dead-letter table; replays resume from the failed step idempotently.

files.purpose: Persisted step pointer, retry schedule with backoff, and a dead-letter table so nothing is dropped
Resumes from current_step, persists progress per step, and hands failures to the retry scheduler instead of dropping
Jittered exponential backoff with a cap; after 6 attempts the saga is dead-lettered, never lost
Claims due retries across replicas with SKIP LOCKED and resumes each saga from its persisted step

migration_strategy: Add the step pointer, retry, and dead-letter tables; backfill current_step from existing completed sagas
Deploy resume-aware runSaga behind SAGA_RETRY=on for new sagas only
Enable the scheduler on a single leader (advisory lock); verify injected failures retry with backoff
Replay the 612 historically lost sagas through the new path with idempotency keys
Add alerting on saga_dead_letter inserts and on retries older than the cap
Ramp to all sagas; remove the fire-once code path after a clean week
testing_strategy: Fixture saga with 3 steps; step 2 fails the first 3 attempts then succeeds. Assert output: saga completes, step 1 ran once (idempotency key recorded once-effective), step 2 retried with increasing next_run_at gaps. Side-effect assert: provider called for step 2 exactly once effectively (idempotency key dedupes the partial successes). DLQ/chaos test: make step 2 fail permanently; assert after 6 attempts a saga_dead_letter row appears with failed_step=1 and the saga is not lost. Resume test: replay the DLQ item; assert it resumes at step 2 (not re-running step 1) and completes. Concurrency test: two scheduler replicas; assert SKIP LOCKED prevents double execution of the same saga.
what_breaks_naive_approach: During the 20-minute provider outage, the single execute() throws, the exception bubbles past the loop, and the saga is dropped with only a log - the customer paid but is never provisioned, exactly the 612 lost executions.

title: Stop double-processing in a FastAPI background task with a Postgres advisory-lock claim
business_context: A devtools CI platform runs a 'finalize build' background task triggered by a webhook. Under load the same build webhook fires twice (provider at-least-once), and two FastAPI workers finalize the same build concurrently, uploading artifacts twice and corrupting the build manifest. 230 builds had duplicated artifacts last month, breaking deterministic-cache assumptions.
constraints: Webhook provider delivers at-least-once; duplicates can arrive within milliseconds
Finalize must run exactly once per build_id across 6 uvicorn workers
Finalize is long-running (up to 90s); a crashed worker must release the claim
No external lock service (Redis) available in this environment; Postgres only
Artifact upload is non-idempotent on the object store
Response to the webhook must be fast (<2s); finalize runs in background

before_state.description: The webhook spawns a background finalize with no concurrency guard; duplicate webhooks run two finalizes for the same build.

files.content:
from fastapi import BackgroundTasks

@app.post('/webhook/build')
async def build_webhook(payload: dict, bg: BackgroundTasks):
    bg.add_task(finalize_build, payload['build_id'])
    return {'accepted': True}

async def finalize_build(build_id: str):
    artifacts = await collect_artifacts(build_id)
    await object_store.upload(build_id, artifacts)  # runs twice on duplicate webhook
    await db.execute('UPDATE builds SET status=$1 WHERE id=$2', 'finalized', build_id)
issues: No dedupe; duplicate webhooks finalize the same build twice
Concurrent finalizes upload artifacts twice, corrupting the manifest
Status update is not gated on a claim; both workers mark finalized

reasoning: Naive fix: check builds.status != 'finalized' before running. Rejected because two workers both read 'building' before either writes (TOCTOU) and both proceed. Alternative: a unique row in a 'finalizing' table. Rejected because if the worker crashes mid-finalize the row stays and the build is never finalized (no lease release). Chosen path: a Postgres session-level advisory lock keyed on a hash of build_id - pg_try_advisory_lock returns false for the second concurrent worker, and the lock is automatically released when the session/connection ends (covering crashes). Combined with a final status check, only one worker finalizes; the other exits immediately. Cross-layer: webhook -> background task acquires pg_try_advisory_lock(hash(build_id)) -> if false, exit -> finalize + status under lock -> connection close releases lock on crash -> config dedicates a connection -> tests assert single finalize under concurrent duplicates.

after_state.description: Finalize acquires a transaction/session advisory lock for the build; only one worker proceeds, and a crash releases the lock automatically.

files.content:
import hashlib

@app.post('/webhook/build')
async def build_webhook(payload: dict, bg: BackgroundTasks):
    bg.add_task(finalize_build, payload['build_id'])
    return {'accepted': True}

def _lock_key(build_id: str) -> int:
    h = hashlib.sha256(build_id.encode()).digest()
    return int.from_bytes(h[:8], 'big', signed=True)  # 64-bit advisory lock key

async def finalize_build(build_id: str):
    async with engine.connect() as conn:  # dedicated session = lock auto-released on close/crash
        got = await conn.scalar(text('SELECT pg_try_advisory_lock(:k)'), {'k': _lock_key(build_id)})
        if not got:
            return  # another worker holds the claim; duplicate webhook exits
        try:
            row = await conn.execute(text('SELECT status FROM builds WHERE id=:id'), {'id': build_id})
            if row.scalar() == 'finalized':
                return  # already done by a prior run
            artifacts = await collect_artifacts(build_id)
            await object_store.upload(build_id, artifacts)
            await conn.execute(text('UPDATE builds SET status=:s WHERE id=:id'), {'s': 'finalized', 'id': build_id})
            await conn.commit()
        finally:
            await conn.execute(text('SELECT pg_advisory_unlock(:k)'), {'k': _lock_key(build_id)})
files.purpose: Session advisory lock gives single-finalizer guarantee; auto-release on connection close handles crashed workers

files.content:
async def upload(build_id: str, artifacts: list):
    # write to a build-scoped, content-addressed path; overwrite is safe since lock guarantees single writer
    key = f'builds/{build_id}/manifest.json'
    await s3.put_object(Bucket=BUCKET, Key=key, Body=serialize(artifacts), IfNoneMatch='*')  # fail if exists
files.purpose: Defense-in-depth: conditional put (IfNoneMatch) rejects a second writer even if the lock were bypassed

migration_strategy: Deploy advisory-lock finalize behind FINALIZE_LOCK=on for one project
Verify duplicate webhooks (replayed in staging) result in a single finalize
Add the conditional S3 put as defense-in-depth and confirm it rejects double writes
Ramp to all projects; monitor lock-not-acquired counter as a duplicate signal
Add an alert if a build stays 'building' beyond 5 minutes (possible crashed holder)
Remove the unguarded path after a clean week of zero duplicated artifacts
testing_strategy: Fixture with real Postgres and a fake object store counting uploads per build. Fire two finalize_build calls for the same build_id concurrently. Assert output: both return without error. Side-effect assert: object_store.upload called exactly once, build status='finalized' once. Crash test: acquire the lock in one connection, kill that connection (simulate worker crash), then run a second finalize; assert it acquires the lock (auto-released) and finalizes once. Negative test: with the conditional put, force a second upload past the lock; assert IfNoneMatch rejects it. Concurrency test: 10 concurrent duplicate webhooks; assert exactly one finalize and no manifest corruption.
what_breaks_naive_approach: The status pre-check is a TOCTOU race: two workers both read status='building', both proceed to upload artifacts to the same build path, and the manifest is written twice - the 230 corrupted builds.

title: Implement exactly-once payment capture across two microservices with a coordinator + outbox
business_context: A fintech checkout splits 'authorize' (auth-service) and 'capture' (capture-service). The checkout orchestrator calls capture over HTTP after auth; on a timeout it retries, sometimes double-capturing because the first capture actually succeeded. 410 double-captures in a quarter triggered customer disputes and processor fines totaling $31k.
constraints: Capture must be exactly-once per authorization despite HTTP retries/timeouts
Services are independently deployed; no shared transaction
Capture-service processor call is non-idempotent unless given an idempotency key
Orchestrator and capture-service each run 4+ replicas
Capture result must be reliably propagated back even if the orchestrator restarts
Auth and capture must reconcile to a single consistent payment state

before_state.description: Orchestrator POSTs capture and retries on timeout. Capture-service processes each request as new. No idempotency key, no result store.

issues: Retry on timeout re-sends capture; first call may have already captured -> double capture
No idempotency key passed to capture-service or processor
No durable record of capture result if orchestrator restarts mid-retry
Each request triggers a fresh processor capture; retried requests double-capture
No unique constraint to dedupe on authId
Result not keyed by an idempotency token the client can safely retry

reasoning: Naive fix: orchestrator stops retrying on timeout. Rejected because then a genuinely lost capture never completes - the order is stuck paid-not-captured. Alternative: capture-service dedupes by authId with a unique constraint. Rejected as incomplete because a legitimate re-capture of a different amount or a re-authorized payment shares authId; we need a per-attempt idempotency key. Chosen path: orchestrator generates a stable idempotency key per logical capture and passes it on every retry; capture-service stores the key with a UNIQUE constraint and returns the stored result on duplicate, and forwards the same key to the processor. The orchestrator persists the capture intent in an outbox so a restart resumes the retry with the same key. Cross-layer: orchestrator generates key + outbox intent -> retry posts same key -> capture-service unique-key insert gates the processor call -> processor idempotency key -> stored result replayed on dup -> orchestrator records final state -> config sets retry/timeout -> tests assert single capture under retries+restart.

after_state.description: A stable idempotency key flows from orchestrator through capture-service to the processor. Capture-service dedupes on the key and replays the stored result; the orchestrator persists intent so restarts reuse the key.

files.purpose: Generates and persists a stable idempotency key so every retry (even post-restart) uses the same key
Unique idem_key gates the processor call once; duplicates replay the stored ref instead of re-capturing
Orchestrator intent store and capture ledger keyed by idempotency key for cross-service exactly-once

migration_strategy: Deploy capture-service idem_key handling; accept but don't require the header (legacy still works) behind CAPTURE_IDEM=on
Roll orchestrator to always send the persisted Idempotency-Key
Enable processor idempotency keys in capture-service
Flip capture-service to require the header; reject header-less after orchestrator is fully rolled
Backfill capture_intents for in-flight authorizations to avoid duplicate keys during cutover
Remove the non-idempotent legacy path after a clean two-week dispute window
testing_strategy: Two-service integration fixture with a fake processor counting captures by idempotency key. Orchestrator captures with a forced timeout on the first attempt (but the capture-service actually processed it). Assert output: final ref returned. Side-effect assert: processor.capture called exactly once for the key, exactly one captures row status='done'. Restart chaos test: kill the orchestrator after the first (successful but timed-out) attempt; restart and re-run checkout; assert ensureCaptureIntent returns the same idem_key and no second capture occurs. Concurrency test: two orchestrator replicas race the same authId; assert one intent, one key, one capture. Negative test: a second request with the same key while status='pending' returns 409, not a new capture.
what_breaks_naive_approach: On a timeout the orchestrator retries with no shared key; the capture-service treats the retry as a brand-new request and calls the non-idempotent processor again - the first capture already succeeded, so the customer is double-captured, the source of the $31k in fines.

title: Guarantee event ordering per aggregate in a partitioned Kafka consumer
business_context: An IoT platform ingests device state events into Kafka. Events are keyed by random UUID instead of device_id, so two updates for the same device land on different partitions and are consumed out of order by 6 parallel consumers, leaving devices showing stale state. A firmware rollout produced 18,000 devices stuck displaying old status, triggering false 'offline' alerts.
constraints: Events for the same device must be applied in production order (monotonic state_version)
Throughput is 40k events/sec across the fleet; parallelism must stay high
Cannot reduce to a single partition (kills throughput)
Consumers are 6 replicas; rebalances must not reorder applied state
Late/out-of-order events must be dropped, not applied
Backward compatible with the existing device_state table

before_state.description: Producer keys events by a random id, scattering same-device events across partitions; consumer applies whatever arrives.

issues: Random partition key splits a device's events across partitions, destroying per-device order
No ordering guarantee at the consumer
Stale events can overwrite newer state
Applies events unconditionally; an older event can overwrite a newer one
No version guard
Out-of-order partitions make last-write-wins incorrect

reasoning: Naive fix: sort events in the consumer before applying. Rejected because events arrive across 6 partitions and consumers independently - there's no global buffer to sort, and buffering to reorder across partitions reintroduces unbounded latency. Alternative: single partition for global ordering. Rejected because it caps throughput far below 40k/sec. Chosen path: key events by device_id so all of a device's events map to one partition (Kafka guarantees per-partition order), AND add a state_version guard in the consumer so even a residual reorder (during the migration or a rare retry) can't apply stale state - the UPDATE only applies when incoming version > stored version. Cross-layer: producer keys by device_id -> Kafka per-partition order -> consumer conditional UPDATE on version -> stale events become no-ops -> config keeps 6 partitions -> migration drains old random-keyed topic -> tests assert ordered apply and stale-drop.

after_state.description: Events are keyed by device_id (per-partition order) and the consumer applies only if the event version exceeds the stored version, making stale events no-ops.

files.purpose: Partition by device_id so Kafka preserves per-device event order without a single partition

files.content: ALTER TABLE device_state ADD COLUMN state_version BIGINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0;
files.purpose: Monotonic version per device so the consumer can reject out-of-order/stale applies
Conditional update applies only newer versions, defending against any residual reordering during migration

migration_strategy: Add state_version column; consumer starts writing it from a new event field (producers emit version) on the existing topic
Stand up device.state.v2 topic; producers dual-publish to old (random key) and new (device_id key) behind ORDERED_TOPIC=on
Switch consumers to read v2 with the version guard; verify stale-dropped metric reflects only true reorders
Drain and stop publishing to the old random-keyed topic once v2 lag is zero
Remove dual-publish; v2 keyed by device_id is the sole path
Delete the old topic and the dual-publish flag after a clean week
testing_strategy: Embedded Kafka + Postgres. Fixture publishes, for one device, versions [1,2,3] but injects them out of order [2,1,3] across the test harness. Assert output: device_state.status reflects version 3 and never regresses to version 1's status. Side-effect assert: state_version ends at 3; the version-1 apply is counted in stale_dropped. Ordering test: with device_id keying, publish 1000 events for 100 devices; assert each device's final version equals its max emitted version. Chaos test: trigger a consumer rebalance mid-stream; assert no device regresses in version. Negative test: replay an old version-1 event after version-3 is applied; assert rowCount=0 and no state change.
what_breaks_naive_approach: With random keys, version 3 and version 1 for one device land on different partitions consumed by different replicas; version 1 can be applied after version 3, overwriting fresh state with stale - the 18,000 devices stuck showing old status and falsely alerting offline.

title: Make a NestJS order-cancellation refund saga compensate idempotently
business_context: A retail platform's cancel flow refunds the payment, restocks inventory, and revokes loyalty points. Each is a separate service call with no coordination; partial failures left customers refunded but with points still spent, or restocked without refund. 280 inconsistent cancellations/month required manual finance reconciliation.
constraints: Three side effects (refund, restock, revoke points) must all-or-nothing per cancellation
Each downstream call may be retried and must be idempotent
Refund provider may take up to 5s; restock and revoke are fast
Cancellation runs on 3 NestJS instances; a retried cancel must not double-refund
If refund succeeds but a later step fails, the cancel must still converge (forward-recover, not roll back the refund)
Saga state persisted; survives restarts

before_state.description: Cancel calls three services sequentially. A failure mid-way leaves inconsistent state and retries re-run completed steps.

issues: No coordination; partial failure leaves refund/restock/points inconsistent
Retry re-runs refund -> double refund
No persisted progress; a restart loses which steps completed

reasoning: Naive fix: try/catch and roll back the refund on later failure. Rejected because refunds are slow and rolling back a refund (re-charging the customer) is a terrible UX and often impossible; the right model is forward-recovery, not compensation, for an already-refunded cancel. Alternative: fire all three in parallel and hope. Rejected because partial failures multiply and there's no ordering or recovery. Chosen path: a persisted, step-tracked saga where each step is idempotent (refund keyed by orderId so a retry is a no-op at the provider, restock guarded by a restocked flag, revoke guarded by a revoked flag). The saga always drives forward to completion (retry failed steps) rather than rolling back the refund. State in Postgres so a restart resumes. Cross-layer: cancel -> persist saga -> idempotent refund (provider key=orderId) -> idempotent restock (flag) -> idempotent revoke (flag) -> markCancelled when all done -> retry worker resumes -> config sets step timeout -> tests assert convergence and no double-refund.

after_state.description: A persisted saga drives the three idempotent steps forward to completion. Retries are no-ops on completed steps; restarts resume from persisted progress.

files.purpose: Each step is guarded by a persisted flag and an idempotency key, so retries skip completed steps and never double-refund
Persisted saga flags survive restarts; upsert is idempotent so a retried cancel reuses the same saga
Saga progress entity; per-step booleans make each step independently resumable
Resumes incomplete cancellations after a crash; safe because every step is idempotent

migration_strategy: Make downstream refund/restock/revoke accept an idempotency key (deploy clients + servers) behind CANCEL_IDEM=on
Add the CancelSaga table; deploy the step-tracked cancel for new cancellations only
Run a one-off reconciliation to fix the historical inconsistent cancellations
Enable the recovery cron on a single instance (leader election)
Ramp the new cancel path to 100% and monitor saga 'completed=false older than 5m' alert
Remove the sequential no-saga path after a clean two weeks
testing_strategy: Fixture with three fake downstream services honoring idempotency keys. Happy path: cancel; assert all three flags true, order cancelled, each downstream called once. Partial-failure test: make loyalty.revoke fail twice then succeed; assert refund and restock are NOT re-run (flags already set, no double-refund) and the saga converges. Restart chaos test: kill the process after refunded=true; run recovery; assert it resumes at restock without re-refunding. Side-effect assert: payments.refund called exactly once across all retries. Negative test: two concurrent cancels for the same order; assert one saga row, refund once.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A failure after refund but before restock leaves the customer refunded with stock not returned and points still spent; the retry re-runs refund() (no idempotency), double-refunding - the two halves of the 280 monthly inconsistencies.

title: Prevent duplicate signups by enforcing uniqueness atomically instead of check-then-insert
business_context: A B2B SaaS lets users self-register. The signup handler checks if the email exists then inserts. Under a double-click or concurrent OAuth+password signup, two rows for the same email were created, splitting the user's data across two accounts. 95 duplicate accounts over two months caused billing confusion and support merges.
constraints: Email uniqueness must hold under concurrent signups (double-click, multi-tab)
Case-insensitive uniqueness (Alice@x.com == alice@x.com)
Must return a friendly 409 on duplicate, not a 500
Runs on 6 API replicas; no shared in-memory state
Existing users table has some historical case-variant duplicates to clean first
Welcome email/side-effects must fire only for the real first signup

before_state.description: Check-then-insert with a SELECT followed by an INSERT; concurrent requests both pass the check.

issues: Check-then-insert TOCTOU: concurrent signups both pass the SELECT and both insert
Case sensitivity: Alice@ and alice@ both inserted
Welcome email fires for both duplicates

reasoning: Naive fix: add an application-level mutex around signup. Rejected because the 6 replicas don't share a mutex, so concurrent requests on different replicas still race. Alternative: SELECT ... FOR UPDATE on the email. Rejected because there's no row to lock yet (the user doesn't exist), so FOR UPDATE locks nothing. Chosen path: a database UNIQUE constraint on lower(email) via a functional unique index, and an INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING that atomically attempts the insert; rowCount tells us if we are the first. Side-effects (welcome email) fire only when the insert actually created the row. Cross-layer: API -> normalize email -> INSERT ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING -> rowCount==0 => 409 -> welcome email only on insert -> migration cleans case-variant dups first -> config none -> tests assert single account under concurrent signups.

after_state.description: A functional unique index on lower(email) plus an atomic INSERT ON CONFLICT makes signup race-safe; side-effects fire only for the true first insert.

files.purpose: Case-insensitive unique index; pre-clean ensures the index can be created without violation
Atomic insert-or-nothing; only the row creator proceeds, so no duplicate account and one welcome email

migration_strategy: Run the duplicate-cleanup query in a maintenance window; verify no two rows share lower(email)
Create the functional unique index concurrently (CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY) to avoid locking
Deploy ON CONFLICT signup behind SIGNUP_ATOMIC=on for a canary tenant
Verify duplicate signups return 409 and emit a single welcome email
Ramp to 100%; remove the check-then-insert code
Add a monitor that alerts if any lower(email) collision is ever attempted post-rollout
testing_strategy: Fixture with real Postgres. Fire 8 concurrent signups with the same email (mixed case: Alice@x.com, alice@x.com) across 2 app instances. Assert output: exactly one returns success, seven get 409. Side-effect assert: exactly one users row, sendWelcome called exactly once. Negative test: signup with leading/trailing spaces and different case resolves to the same normalized email and is deduped. Chaos test: kill the process between insert and sendWelcome; on retry the duplicate insert returns 409 so no second account is created (welcome may be re-attempted but a separate idempotency guard or the 409 path prevents a second account). Migration test: seed case-variant dups, run cleanup, assert index builds successfully.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Two concurrent signups both run SELECT, both see no existing row, both INSERT - producing two accounts for one email and two welcome emails, the exact 95 duplicate-account incidents.

title: Implement CDC-based outbox using Debezium to decouple event publishing from app code
business_context: A logistics company has 40 microservices each doing dual-writes (DB + Kafka), and bugs in each service's publishing code caused scattered event loss. Instead of fixing 40 outbox relays, the platform team standardizes on a Debezium CDC pipeline reading the outbox table's WAL, so services only write to their DB. A recent incident lost 26,000 events because one service's hand-rolled relay had a bug; the team wants a single, audited pipeline.
constraints: Services write only to Postgres; no service should call Kafka directly
Event publishing must be guaranteed at-least-once from the outbox table
CDC connector must survive Postgres failover and resume from the last committed LSN
Outbox rows must be pruned without losing un-streamed events
Event schema and topic routing derived from outbox columns
Backward compatible: existing consumers keep their topics

before_state.description: Each service has its own polling relay with subtle bugs (missed rows, double-publish on crash, no failover resume).

issues: Per-row update after send: crash leaves published=false and re-sends, or marks published without confirming send
No ordering by LSN; concurrent relays in multiple pods double-publish
No failover resume; on Postgres failover the relay may skip rows committed just before

reasoning: Naive fix: harden each of the 40 relays. Rejected because it multiplies maintenance and every team re-derives subtle correctness bugs. Alternative: a central polling relay service reading every outbox table. Rejected because polling 40 tables at scale is inefficient and still races with pruning and failover-LSN gaps. Chosen path: Debezium CDC reads the Postgres WAL directly, capturing every committed outbox INSERT in commit order with exactly the LSN tracking that handles failover, transforming rows to Kafka events via the outbox SMT (topic/key from columns). Services keep writing the outbox row in their business transaction (unchanged correctness) but delete the relay code. Cross-layer: service writes outbox in tx -> Postgres WAL -> Debezium connector (LSN-tracked) -> outbox event router SMT -> Kafka topic from column -> consumers unchanged -> config defines connector + replication slot -> tests assert every committed row is streamed exactly in order.

after_state.description: Services only write the outbox row transactionally. Debezium streams committed rows from the WAL to Kafka with LSN-based resume; per-service relay code is deleted.

files.purpose: Services only write the outbox row in the same transaction; no Kafka call, no relay to get wrong

files.content:
{
