  "name": "outbox-connector",
  "config": {
    "connector.class": "io.debezium.connector.postgresql.PostgresConnector",
    "plugin.name": "pgoutput",
    "slot.name": "outbox_slot",
    "publication.name": "outbox_pub",
    "table.include.list": "public.outbox",
    "transforms": "outbox",
    "transforms.outbox.type": "io.debezium.transforms.outbox.EventRouter",
    "transforms.outbox.route.by.field": "aggregate_type",
    "transforms.outbox.table.field.event.key": "aggregate_id",
    "transforms.outbox.table.field.event.payload": "payload",
    "heartbeat.interval.ms": "5000"
  }
}
files.purpose: CDC connector reads the outbox table from the WAL with LSN tracking and routes rows to topics by aggregate_type
Logical replication slot + publication give Debezium a durable, ordered, failover-safe read of the outbox

files.content:
-- prune only rows the connector has confirmed streamed (LSN <= confirmed_flush_lsn)
DELETE FROM outbox o
USING pg_replication_slots s
WHERE s.slot_name = 'outbox_slot'
  AND o.created_at < now() - interval '7 days';
files.purpose: Safe pruning that never deletes un-streamed rows because the slot holds the WAL until confirmed

migration_strategy: Create the publication and replication slot; deploy Debezium reading outbox into a parallel '.cdc' topic mirror
Verify the CDC stream matches the hand-rolled relay output 1:1 for one pilot service
Cut consumers of the pilot service over to the CDC-produced topic; keep the relay dark
Delete the relay code in the pilot service; expand to the next batch of services
Enable LSN-aware pruning job; confirm the slot prevents un-streamed deletion
Decommission all 40 hand-rolled relays once every service is on CDC for two weeks
testing_strategy: Integration test with Postgres + Debezium (testcontainers) + Kafka. Fixture writes 1,000 outbox rows across 50 concurrent transactions. Assert output: Kafka receives exactly 1,000 events routed to the correct topic by aggregate_type, in per-aggregate commit order. Side-effect assert: each event key equals aggregate_id; no duplicates, no gaps. Failover chaos test: promote a Postgres replica mid-stream; assert Debezium resumes from confirmed LSN and no committed row is skipped or duplicated beyond at-least-once. Pruning test: prune with a held slot; assert un-streamed rows are never deleted. Negative test: a rolled-back transaction's outbox insert never appears in Kafka (WAL only carries committed rows).
what_breaks_naive_approach: The hand-rolled relay's send-then-update has a crash window and no LSN resume; on Postgres failover it can skip rows committed just before promotion, silently losing events - the 26,000-event incident.

title: Fix non-atomic counter increment in Redis leaderboard with INCR + sorted-set transaction
business_context: A gaming platform tracks live tournament scores in a Redis leaderboard. The scoring service reads a player's score, adds points in the app, and writes it back, plus updates a sorted set. Concurrent score events for the same player lost updates and desynced the counter from the sorted set, so the leaderboard showed wrong rankings during a 50k-player event, causing prize disputes.
constraints: Up to 200 score events/sec per top player during finals
The numeric score and the sorted-set rank must always agree (no desync)
Score deltas can be negative (penalties); must apply correctly
Runs on 8 scoring workers; no app-level locks
Leaderboard reads (top-100) must stay sub-10ms
Idempotent per score event (event_id) to tolerate retried events

before_state.description: Read-modify-write of a string score plus a separate ZADD. Concurrent updates lose increments and desync the two structures.

issues: Read-modify-write loses concurrent increments
score string and leaderboard zset updated in two ops; a crash between desyncs them
No idempotency; a retried score event double-counts

reasoning: Naive fix: use redis.incrby for the score. Rejected as incomplete because INCRBY fixes the counter race but the ZADD is still a separate non-atomic op that can desync, and there's still no idempotency for retried events. Alternative: WATCH/MULTI optimistic transaction. Rejected because under 200 events/sec on a hot player the WATCH retries thrash. Chosen path: a single Lua script that, given an event_id, checks a dedupe set, atomically does INCRBYFLOAT on the score and ZADD the same new value to the sorted set, all in one atomic server-side execution - counter and zset can never desync, and the dedupe set makes it idempotent. Cross-layer: score event -> Lua (dedupe check + INCRBYFLOAT + ZADD atomically) -> idempotent on retry -> reads from zset unchanged -> config sets dedupe TTL -> tests assert counter==zset and no lost updates.

after_state.description: A single Lua script atomically dedupes by event_id, increments the score, and updates the sorted set to the same value, eliminating both the lost-update race and counter/zset desync.

files.content:
-- KEYS[1]=score:player KEYS[2]=leaderboard KEYS[3]=dedupe:player
-- ARGV[1]=delta ARGV[2]=player ARGV[3]=eventId ARGV[4]=dedupeTtl
if redis.call('SISMEMBER', KEYS[3], ARGV[3]) == 1 then
  return tonumber(redis.call('GET', KEYS[1]) or '0') -- already applied: no-op
end
local newscore = redis.call('INCRBYFLOAT', KEYS[1], ARGV[1])
redis.call('ZADD', KEYS[2], newscore, ARGV[2])
redis.call('SADD', KEYS[3], ARGV[3])
redis.call('EXPIRE', KEYS[3], ARGV[4])
return newscore
files.purpose: Atomic dedupe + increment + zset update in one server-side script; counter and ranking can never diverge
Calls the atomic script; reads remain a fast zrevrange so top-100 stays sub-10ms
Durable snapshots so a Redis loss can be reconstructed and prize results are auditable

migration_strategy: Load the Lua script; dual-run new applyScore in shadow comparing results with the old path behind LB_ATOMIC=shadow
Backfill the score keys and zset from the last durable snapshot to ensure consistency at cutover
Switch scoring workers to the atomic script for one low-stakes tournament
Enable periodic Postgres snapshots for audit
Ramp to all tournaments; remove the read-modify-write path
Add an invariant checker comparing each score key to its zset score; alert on any drift, then drop the shadow flag
testing_strategy: Fixture with real Redis. Fire 2,000 concurrent score events (mix of +/- deltas, some duplicate event_ids) for 10 players across 8 simulated workers. Assert output: each player's final score equals the sum of distinct (deduped) deltas. Side-effect assert: for every player, GET score:player == ZSCORE leaderboard player (no desync). Idempotency test: replay 500 events with reused event_ids; assert scores unchanged. Chaos test: kill a worker mid-burst; assert no player's counter and zset disagree and no lost or double increments. Negative test: a penalty taking a score negative is reflected identically in both structures.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Two concurrent score events read the same cur, both write next, losing one increment; and the separate ZADD can run while another worker's SET hasn't, so the counter and the ranking sorted set disagree - the wrong rankings and prize disputes.

title: Add saga timeout + compensation for a stuck 'pending' payment that never resolves
business_context: A fintech wallet top-up saga calls an external bank that sometimes neither confirms nor declines, leaving the saga 'pending' indefinitely. The old code waited synchronously; stuck calls held the user's funds in limbo. 540 top-ups were stuck pending for over 24h last quarter, each a support escalation and a regulatory complaint risk.
constraints: Bank may respond confirm, decline, or never (timeout class)
A pending saga must auto-resolve within a configurable deadline (default 30 min)
On timeout, the saga must query the bank's status endpoint before compensating (avoid double-spend)
Compensation (reverse the hold) must be idempotent
Saga state persisted; workers are 5 replicas
User must be notified of the final outcome exactly once

before_state.description: The saga awaits the bank synchronously with no deadline; a non-responding bank leaves it hung and the row stuck 'pending'.

issues: No timeout on bank.charge; a non-responding bank hangs the saga forever
Stuck 'pending' state never auto-resolves
No status reconciliation; can't safely compensate without knowing if the charge went through

reasoning: Naive fix: add a Promise.race timeout to bank.charge and release funds on timeout. Rejected because a timeout doesn't tell you whether the bank actually charged - releasing funds while the bank charged double-credits. Alternative: just retry charge on timeout. Rejected because re-charging a possibly-already-charged bank double-spends. Chosen path: a persisted saga with a deadline; a sweeper finds sagas past their deadline and, before compensating, queries the bank's status endpoint (keyed by the original idempotency reference) to learn the true outcome - if charged, credit the wallet; if not charged, release the hold idempotently. The user notification is gated by a unique constraint so it fires once. Cross-layer: topUp persists saga + deadline -> async bank.charge with idem ref -> sweeper finds overdue -> bank.status(ref) reconciles truth -> idempotent credit or release -> notify once -> config sets deadline -> tests assert no double-spend on indeterminate bank.

after_state.description: Saga is persisted with a deadline. A sweeper reconciles overdue sagas against the bank's status endpoint and idempotently credits or releases, notifying the user exactly once.

files.purpose: Persisted saga with a deadline and a notified flag for one-time notification
Persists the saga with a deadline and an idempotency ref; on call timeout it leaves the saga for the sweeper
Reconciles overdue sagas against the bank's truth before acting; idempotent credit/release and one-time notify

migration_strategy: Add the topup_sagas table; new top-ups persist a saga and deadline behind TOPUP_SAGA=on
Make holdFunds/creditWallet/releaseFunds accept the bank_ref idempotency key
Enable the sweeper on a single leader; verify injected hung top-ups reconcile via bank.status
Backfill the 540 historically stuck top-ups into the saga table and let the sweeper resolve them
Ramp new top-ups to 100%; alert on any saga pending past 2x deadline
Remove the synchronous-only path after a clean two weeks of zero stuck pendings
testing_strategy: Fixture with a fake bank that can be set to never respond, then later report 'charged' or 'not_charged' on status. Hang test: trigger a top-up where charge never returns; advance past the deadline; run the sweeper. Assert: if bank.status='charged', wallet credited once; if 'not_charged', hold released once; saga state final, user notified exactly once. Side-effect assert: creditWallet/releaseFunds each called at most once (idempotency ref). Chaos test: kill the worker between sweeper claim and resolution; assert the SKIP LOCKED claim releases and another worker reconciles without double action. Negative test: bank actually charged but app thought timeout - assert the status reconciliation credits (no funds lost) and never releases a charged hold.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A naive timeout that releases funds on non-response double-spends when the bank actually charged; and without a deadline+sweeper the saga simply hangs 'pending' forever - the 540 stuck top-ups and regulatory risk.

title: Eliminate phantom reads in availability check with SERIALIZABLE + retry for double-booking
business_context: A B2B meeting-room booking SaaS checks for overlapping bookings then inserts. Two concurrent requests for the same room/time both pass the overlap check and both insert (phantom read), double-booking the room. 64 double-bookings in a month caused embarrassing meeting conflicts and one cancelled client demo.
constraints: Overlap detection must be correct under concurrent booking of the same room
Cannot lock the whole bookings table (other rooms must stay concurrent)
Postgres backend; bookings can span arbitrary time ranges
Retries on serialization failure must be transparent to the client
p95 booking latency under 150ms
Backward compatible booking API

before_state.description: Read overlapping rows under READ COMMITTED then insert; a concurrent transaction inserts a conflicting row the first never saw (phantom).

issues: Check-then-insert under READ COMMITTED allows a phantom: concurrent tx inserts an overlapping row the SELECT didn't see
No exclusion constraint to enforce non-overlap at the DB
Two requests for the same slot both succeed

reasoning: Naive fix: add a uniqueness index on (room_id, start_at). Rejected because overlap is a range condition, not exact equality - two different-but-overlapping ranges aren't a unique-key collision. Alternative: SELECT ... FOR UPDATE on overlapping rows. Rejected because FOR UPDATE locks existing rows but not the gap where a new overlapping row will be inserted (no phantom protection). Chosen path: a Postgres GiST exclusion constraint that makes overlapping ranges for the same room a hard DB-level violation, plus SERIALIZABLE isolation with transparent retry for the rare serialization failure. The exclusion constraint is the real guarantee; SERIALIZABLE + retry handles any remaining anomaly cleanly. Cross-layer: API -> SERIALIZABLE tx -> insert (exclusion constraint enforces non-overlap) -> 23P01 => ConflictError, 40001 => retry -> config sets isolation -> tests assert no double-booking under concurrency.

after_state.description: A GiST exclusion constraint makes overlapping bookings impossible at the DB; the insert under SERIALIZABLE retries transparently on serialization failure and maps exclusion violations to a clean conflict.

files.purpose: DB-level guarantee that no two bookings for the same room can overlap, eliminating phantom double-booking
Inserts under SERIALIZABLE; exclusion violation = clean conflict, serialization failure = transparent retry

migration_strategy: Resolve any existing overlapping bookings in the data (report + manual fix) before adding the constraint
Add btree_gist and the exclusion constraint in a maintenance window (validate not exists overlaps first)
Deploy SERIALIZABLE insert-with-retry behind BOOKING_EXCL=on for a canary tenant
Verify concurrent same-slot bookings return exactly one success
Ramp to 100%; remove the check-then-insert SELECT
Monitor serialization-retry rate; tune if it spikes, then drop the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture with real Postgres. Fire 10 concurrent bookings for the same room and overlapping times via Promise.all. Assert output: exactly one succeeds, nine get ConflictError. Side-effect assert: exactly one bookings row for that slot. Non-overlap test: concurrent bookings for the same room but disjoint times all succeed. Different-room test: concurrent bookings for different rooms at the same time all succeed (no false contention). Chaos test: induce serialization failures by interleaving overlapping inserts; assert the retry loop converges and never produces two overlapping rows. Negative test: attempt to insert a booking overlapping an existing one; assert 23P01 mapped to ConflictError.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Under READ COMMITTED, two concurrent transactions both run the overlap SELECT (each seeing no conflict), then both INSERT - the phantom row each missed becomes a real double-booking, the 64 monthly conflicts.

title: Coordinate cross-shard order + payment with a two-phase saga across sharded Postgres
business_context: A high-scale marketplace shards orders by buyer and payments by a separate payment-account shard, so an order and its payment live in different Postgres clusters with no cross-cluster transaction. The old code wrote order then payment; cross-shard partial failures left orders with no payment record or vice versa. 1,300 reconciliation mismatches/month consumed a full-time analyst.
constraints: Order shard and payment shard are separate Postgres clusters; no distributed XA
Order and payment must be eventually consistent and reconcilable to one state
Each shard write must be idempotent under retry
A failure on the second shard must not leave the first committed-and-orphaned
Throughput 6k orders/min; latency budget 300ms p95
Reconciliation must converge automatically, not via a human

before_state.description: Sequential writes to two clusters; a failure on the second leaves the first orphaned with no rollback path.

issues: No cross-shard atomicity; second write failing orphans the first
No idempotency; a retry double-inserts on the cluster that succeeded
No reconciliation to converge the two clusters

reasoning: Naive fix: write both then on payment failure delete the order. Rejected because the delete can itself fail (or the process crash), leaving the orphan, and a deleted order may already have downstream effects. Alternative: distributed 2PC/XA across the two clusters. Rejected because managed Postgres clusters here don't share an XA coordinator and 2PC blocks under coordinator failure. Chosen path: a saga with a 'pending' intent written to a coordinator store first, then idempotent writes to each shard keyed by a saga_id, then a 'committed' marker; a reconciler scans intents older than a threshold and either completes the missing shard write (forward recovery) or marks the order void if payment can't be created. Each shard write uses INSERT ON CONFLICT(saga_id) for idempotency. Cross-layer: checkout writes intent -> idempotent order insert -> idempotent payment insert -> mark committed -> reconciler completes/voids stragglers -> config sets reconcile interval -> tests assert convergence and no orphan.

after_state.description: A coordinator intent precedes idempotent per-shard writes keyed by saga_id; a reconciler forward-completes or voids stragglers so the two clusters always converge.

files.purpose: Coordinator record of intent so a reconciler knows what cross-shard work must converge
Idempotent per-shard inserts keyed by saga_id; the coordinator intent gates reconciliation
Drives stragglers to a single consistent outcome: complete the missing write or void, no orphans

migration_strategy: Add saga_id columns + ON CONFLICT(saga_id) to orders and payments tables; backfill nulls allowed
Stand up the coordinator intents table; write intents behind XSHARD_SAGA=on for new checkouts
Deploy idempotent inserts; verify replays don't double-insert on either shard
Enable the reconciler on a single leader; inject failed second-shard writes and confirm convergence
Run the reconciler over the historical mismatch backlog once
Ramp to 100%; retire the sequential code and the analyst's manual process after a clean month
testing_strategy: Fixture with two Postgres clusters (testcontainers). Happy path: checkout; assert order row on shard A, payment row on shard B, intent state='committed'. Partial-failure test: make the payment insert throw; assert intent stays 'pending', order exists alone, then run reconcile -> assert payment insert is forward-completed and intent='committed'. Reverse test: make the order insert throw after payment landed; reconcile voids the payment. Idempotency test: replay checkout with the same saga_id x3; assert one order, one payment. Chaos test: kill the process between order and payment insert; assert the reconciler completes it. Side-effect assert: no saga ever ends with exactly one of the two rows.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Cluster A commits the order, then the cluster B payment insert fails or the process crashes; there's no transaction spanning clusters and no reconciler, so the order is permanently orphaned - the 1,300 monthly mismatches.

title: Make a Go job worker idempotent with a claimed-and-completed job ledger
business_context: A devtools platform runs a Go worker pool pulling jobs from a queue. At-least-once delivery means a job can be delivered twice (visibility-timeout expiry while still processing), and the worker ran the side effect (provisioning a sandbox) twice, leaking cloud resources. $14k of duplicate sandboxes were billed last quarter.
constraints: Queue delivers at-least-once; the same job_id can arrive twice concurrently
Side effect (sandbox provisioning) is non-idempotent and costly
Workers are 20 goroutines across 4 pods
A worker crash mid-job must let another worker safely re-claim it
Job must be marked done only after the side effect commits
Postgres for the ledger; no Redis

before_state.description: The worker processes any delivered job; no claim or completion ledger, so a redelivered job re-provisions.

issues: No dedupe on job_id; a redelivered job provisions a second sandbox
Provision happens before the ledger write, so a crash after provision re-provisions on retry
No claim; two workers can process the same job concurrently

reasoning: Naive fix: check if a sandbox row exists before provisioning. Rejected because two concurrent redeliveries both read 'no row' (TOCTOU) and both provision. Alternative: rely on the queue's exactly-once mode. Rejected because this queue only offers at-least-once and visibility-timeout expiry will redeliver. Chosen path: a job_ledger row inserted with UNIQUE(job_id) in a 'claimed' state inside a transaction - the unique insert is the atomic claim; only the claimer provisions, then transitions the row to 'done' with the sandbox id. A redelivery loses the unique insert and either returns the existing result or, if still 'claimed' and stale, is allowed to re-claim after a lease timeout (handling crashed workers). Cross-layer: queue -> claim via unique insert -> provision once -> mark done with sandbox id -> redelivery short-circuits -> lease reclaim on crash -> config sets lease -> tests assert single provision under concurrent redelivery.

after_state.description: A unique job_ledger claim gates provisioning. Only the claimer provisions; redeliveries short-circuit; a lease timeout lets another worker re-claim a crashed job.

files.content:
CREATE TABLE job_ledger (
  job_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'claimed',
  sandbox_id TEXT,
  claimed_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  worker_id TEXT NOT NULL
);
files.purpose: Idempotency + claim ledger; PRIMARY KEY on job_id makes the claim atomic, claimed_at enables lease reclaim
Atomic claim via unique insert; only the owner provisions, redeliveries ack-and-skip, stale claims reclaimable
Detects abandoned claims so a crashed worker's job can be safely re-claimed without manual intervention

migration_strategy: Deploy the job_ledger table; run process with claim logic in 'observe' mode logging would-be skips behind JOB_IDEM=on
Backfill ledger rows for in-flight jobs to avoid re-provisioning during cutover
Enable enforcing claims for one job type; verify redeliveries skip provisioning
Tune lease timeout above the queue's visibility timeout to avoid premature reclaim
Ramp to all job types; remove the unguarded path
Add an alert on jobs stuck 'claimed' beyond 2x lease (possible crash storm), then drop the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture with a fake cloud counting Provision calls and a real Postgres. Deliver the same job_id to two goroutines concurrently. Assert output: both return nil. Side-effect assert: cloud.Provision called exactly once, one job_ledger row status='done', one sandbox row. Crash/reclaim test: a worker claims then 'crashes' (no done); advance past the lease; redeliver; assert another worker reclaims and provisions exactly once total (because the first never provisioned). Done-skip test: redeliver a done job; assert it acks without provisioning. Negative test: with lease set high, a concurrent redelivery of a still-claimed job must skip, not provision.
what_breaks_naive_approach: The visibility timeout expires while the worker is still provisioning; the queue redelivers, a second worker reads no sandbox row, and provisions again - the duplicate sandboxes and $14k cloud bill.

title: Fix write-skew on shared budget approval with SERIALIZABLE in a fintech approvals flow
business_context: A corporate-card platform requires two approvers but enforces a department budget cap. Two managers concurrently approve two expenses; each checks remaining budget (sufficient for its own expense) and approves, but together they exceed the cap - a write-skew anomaly. Departments overspent by a combined $210k in a quarter before finance noticed.
constraints: Concurrent approvals against the same department budget must not jointly exceed the cap
Each approval reads remaining budget then inserts an approval+charge
Cannot serialize all approvals globally (high volume across departments)
Postgres backend; approvals run on 6 replicas
Approvals must be idempotent on retry
Backward compatible approval API

before_state.description: Each approval reads SUM(approved) under READ COMMITTED then inserts; two concurrent approvals each see budget room and both commit (write skew).

issues: Write-skew: two concurrent approvals read the same 'spent', both pass, both commit, exceeding the cap
Read and the two writes are not in a transaction
No idempotency; a retried approval double-charges the budget

reasoning: Naive fix: SELECT FOR UPDATE the dept_budgets row to serialize approvers. Rejected because the constraint forbids globally serializing all approvals and FOR UPDATE on the budget row does exactly that for the department but the real subtlety is the anomaly is write-skew across the SUM, which FOR UPDATE on the single budget row actually does prevent - however the team also needs idempotency and wants the DB to enforce the invariant declaratively. We choose a combination: a CHECK-backed atomic UPDATE that decrements remaining budget in one statement (UPDATE ... SET spent=spent+amount WHERE spent+amount<=cap) so the cap check and the write are atomic, plus SERIALIZABLE with retry for the approval+charge insert pairing, plus an idempotency key. Rejected pure-SERIALIZABLE-only because the conditional UPDATE gives a cheaper, lock-scoped guarantee per department. Cross-layer: API -> conditional UPDATE (atomic cap check+spend) -> 0 rows => over budget -> approval insert idempotent -> SERIALIZABLE wraps the pair -> config sets retry -> tests assert joint cap respected.

after_state.description: Budget spend is a single atomic conditional UPDATE (cap check + spend together), wrapped in a SERIALIZABLE transaction with retry and an idempotency guard, so concurrent approvals cannot jointly exceed the cap.

files.purpose: DB CHECK makes overspend impossible even if app logic regresses; unique idem_key dedupes retries
Atomic cap-check+spend plus idempotent approval under SERIALIZABLE with retry; write-skew impossible

migration_strategy: Add the CHECK constraint (validate existing spent<=cap first) and the idem_key unique index
Require an Idempotency-Key from approval clients
Deploy the atomic conditional-UPDATE approval behind APPROVAL_ATOMIC=on for one department
Load-test concurrent approvals against the cap; verify joint spend never exceeds cap
Ramp to 100%; remove the read-then-write code path
Add a daily reconciliation: SUM(approvals.amount) per dept == spent; alert on drift, then drop the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: dept cap=1000, spent=800. Fire two concurrent approvals of 150 each via Promise.all under SERIALIZABLE. Assert output: exactly one succeeds, the other gets OverBudgetError (800+150+150=1100 > cap). Side-effect assert: spent ends at 950, CHECK never violated, exactly one approval row inserted for the rejected one's idem? (rejected one rolls back). Write-skew test: 5 concurrent approvals of 60 against cap room of 200; assert at most 3 succeed (180<=200) and spend never exceeds cap. Idempotency test: same idem_key twice concurrently; assert one approval, budget charged once. Chaos test: inject serialization failures; assert retries converge without overspend.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Two managers' approvals both read spent=800 under READ COMMITTED, each computes 950<=1000 and commits, leaving spent=1100 over the cap - the write-skew that overspent departments by $210k.

title: Replace fire-and-forget gRPC inventory sync with a transactional outbox + retry stream
business_context: A logistics warehouse service updates local stock then fire-and-forgets a gRPC call to a central inventory service. When the central service was redeployed (30s downtime), 9,400 stock updates were dropped, so the central view diverged from warehouses for hours, causing oversells on the storefront. The team needs guaranteed propagation.
constraints: Every local stock change must propagate to central exactly-once-in-effect
Central gRPC may be down for minutes; updates must not be lost
Updates carry a per-SKU version; central must apply in order and reject stale
Warehouse service runs 8 instances; the propagation relay must not double-send
Local stock write must stay fast (<25ms)
Backward compatible central API (UpdateStock RPC unchanged)

before_state.description: Local update commits, then a best-effort gRPC call is made without awaiting durability; downtime drops the update.

issues: Fire-and-forget gRPC: any central downtime silently drops the update
No retry/durability for the propagation
No version sent; central can't reject stale or reorder

reasoning: Naive fix: await the gRPC and retry in a loop. Rejected because a process crash during the loop loses the update and a multi-minute central outage exhausts in-process retries. Alternative: queue updates in memory and flush. Rejected because in-memory queues die with the pod, losing buffered updates. Chosen path: write the local stock change AND an outbox row atomically; a relay streams outbox rows to central via the UpdateStock RPC (with version) using SKIP LOCKED across instances, marking rows published only after a successful ack; central applies version-guarded (rejects stale). Cross-layer: update -> local UPDATE + outbox (one tx) -> relay claims rows SKIP LOCKED -> gRPC UpdateStock(version) -> central version guard -> mark published on ack -> config sets relay batch -> tests assert no lost update across central downtime.

after_state.description: Local change and an outbox row commit atomically. A relay streams to central with version, marking rows published only after ack; central applies in version order and rejects stale.

files.purpose: Durable outbox of stock changes with version for ordered, stale-safe propagation
Writes the stock change and the propagation event atomically so central updates cannot be lost
Streams outbox rows to central with version, only marking published after ack; SKIP LOCKED avoids double-send
Central applies only newer versions, making redelivered or out-of-order updates safe no-ops

migration_strategy: Add the stock_outbox table and version columns; dual-run fire-and-forget plus outbox write behind STOCK_OUTBOX=shadow
Verify outbox rows match local stock changes 1:1 in staging
Enable the relay on a canary instance; central applies version-guarded (idempotent against dual emit)
Disable the fire-and-forget call; relay becomes the sole propagation path; ramp relay to all instances
Replay the historically dropped updates from a recovery export through the relay
Remove fire-and-forget and the shadow flag after central/warehouse drift is zero for a week
testing_strategy: Fixture with a real Postgres and a fake central gRPC server. Update 500 SKUs locally; run the relay; assert central qty matches local for all SKUs. Side-effect assert: every outbox row has published_at set; central applied each version once. Downtime chaos test: take the central server offline for 10s during updates; assert outbox rows stay unpublished, then drain and converge once central returns - zero lost updates. Ordering test: emit versions [1,2,3] for a SKU but deliver out of order; assert central ends at version 3 and rejects the stale version-1 apply. Idempotency test: re-run the relay over already-published rows manually re-marked unpublished; assert central's version guard makes the replays no-ops.
what_breaks_naive_approach: During the 30s central redeploy, every fire-and-forget gRPC call fails and is swallowed by the empty catch; the local stock change is committed but never propagates, diverging central from warehouses - the 9,400 dropped updates and resulting oversells.

title: Make Next.js server action checkout idempotent against React double-submit
business_context: A Next.js e-commerce app uses a server action for checkout. React Strict Mode and impatient users double-fire the action, and the action both charges and creates an order each time. During a promo, 480 customers were charged twice within seconds, generating a refund storm and 1-star reviews.
constraints: Server action may be invoked twice within ~200ms for one logical checkout
Charge (Stripe) is non-idempotent without a key
Action runs on Vercel serverless; instances are ephemeral, no shared memory
Must work without changing the existing order schema's primary key
Idempotency token must originate client-side and survive the double-submit
Order confirmation must render exactly one order

before_state.description: The server action charges then inserts an order on every invocation, with no idempotency token.

issues: No idempotency token; a double-submit charges twice and creates two orders
Charge precedes the order insert, so the dedupe point is missing
Ephemeral serverless instances can't dedupe in memory

reasoning: Naive fix: disable the submit button after click. Rejected because it doesn't stop Strict Mode double-invocation, programmatic resubmits, or a retried server action, and is purely client UX. Alternative: dedupe by cartId (one order per cart). Rejected because a cart can legitimately be re-checked-out after a failed attempt, and concurrent double-submits both read 'no order for cart' (TOCTOU). Chosen path: a client-generated idempotency token embedded in the form (stable across the double-submit via useRef), claimed server-side with a UNIQUE insert before charging; the charge uses the token as Stripe's idempotency key, and the order is created in the same transaction. A duplicate invocation loses the unique insert and returns the stored order. Cross-layer: client useRef token -> hidden form field -> server action claims token (unique) -> Stripe charge with idempotency key -> order insert in tx -> duplicate replays stored order -> config none -> tests assert single charge under double-submit.

after_state.description: A stable client token is claimed server-side via a unique insert before charging; Stripe uses it as the idempotency key; duplicates replay the stored order.

files.purpose: Generates one idempotency token per mounted checkout that survives React double-submission
Claims the token atomically before charging; Stripe idempotency key + stored order make duplicates safe
Idempotency ledger linking the client token to the resulting order for replay

migration_strategy: Add the checkout_keys table; ship the form change emitting an idempotency token
Deploy the token-claiming server action behind a flag accepting missing tokens as before
Verify in preview that Strict Mode double-invocation results in one charge
Require the token; reject token-less checkouts once the client is fully rolled
Add a sweeper deleting checkout_keys without an order_id older than 1h (abandoned)
Remove the legacy charge-first path after a clean promo with zero double charges
testing_strategy: Fixture stubs Stripe to count charges by idempotency key and adds latency. Invoke the server action twice concurrently with the same token (simulating Strict Mode). Assert output: both redirect to the same order id. Side-effect assert: Stripe charge called once for the key, one orders row, one checkout_keys row with order_id set. Negative test: two different tokens for the same cart create two charges (legitimate re-checkout). Chaos test: kill the function after the charge but before the order insert; on the client retry with the same token, the Stripe idempotency key returns the same charge and reconciliation links the order without a second charge.
what_breaks_naive_approach: React Strict Mode (or an impatient user) fires the server action twice within 200ms; with no token both invocations call the non-idempotent Stripe charge and insert two orders - the 480 double charges.

title: Add a poison-message dead-letter to a Kafka consumer so one bad event can't block the partition
business_context: A social platform's notification consumer crashes on a malformed event (null user_id), and because it doesn't commit the offset, Kafka redelivers the same poison message forever, blocking the entire partition. One bad event halted notifications for 1.2M users for 40 minutes until an engineer manually skipped the offset.
constraints: A single un-processable event must not block the partition
Poison events must be routed to a DLQ topic with full context for later inspection
At-least-once for good events must be preserved (no silent drops of valid events)
Consumer runs 12 partitions across 4 pods
Retryable (transient) errors must be retried; non-retryable (validation) must DLQ immediately
Offset must advance past a DLQ'd event

before_state.description: Any processing error throws, the offset isn't committed, and the same message is redelivered indefinitely, head-of-line blocking the partition.

issues: A validation error throws and the offset is never committed -> infinite redelivery blocks the partition
No distinction between transient and permanent errors
No DLQ; bad events have nowhere to go and halt everything behind them

reasoning: Naive fix: wrap eachMessage in try/catch and swallow errors. Rejected because swallowing also drops transient failures (a brief downstream outage) silently, violating at-least-once for good events. Alternative: a fixed retry count then skip. Rejected as too blunt - a transient outage longer than the retry window would drop valid events, and a poison event still wastes N retries head-of-line blocking. Chosen path: classify errors - transient errors retry with bounded backoff (then DLQ if exhausted), non-retryable validation errors DLQ immediately; the offset always advances after either successful processing or DLQ routing, so the partition never head-of-line blocks. The DLQ message carries the original payload, error, and source offset. Cross-layer: consume -> validate/process -> classify error -> retry transient / DLQ permanent -> commit offset past it -> DLQ topic for inspection -> config sets max retries -> tests assert poison event DLQ'd and partition progresses.

after_state.description: Errors are classified; transient ones retry with backoff then DLQ, permanent ones DLQ immediately, and the offset always advances so the partition keeps flowing.

files.purpose: Distinguishes permanent (validation/parse) from transient failures to decide retry vs DLQ
Routes permanent failures to the DLQ and lets the offset advance, so one bad event never blocks the partition
Bounded backoff for transient errors; converts exhaustion into a DLQ decision so the partition unblocks

migration_strategy: Create the .dlq topics for each consumed topic
Deploy classified error handling with DLQ routing behind DLQ_ENABLED=on for one consumer group
Replay the historical poison message into staging; verify it lands in the DLQ and the partition advances
Add a DLQ monitor + dashboard and an alert on DLQ rate spikes
Ramp to all consumers; build a DLQ replay tool for fixed events
Remove the throw-forever behavior after a clean week and document the DLQ runbook
testing_strategy: Embedded Kafka. Fixture publishes good events interleaved with one poison event (null userId). Assert output: all good events processed (notifications sent) and the partition offset advances past the poison message. Side-effect assert: exactly one message in the .dlq topic with x-error and x-source-offset headers. Transient test: make sendNotification fail transiently 3 times then succeed; assert it retries with backoff and is NOT DLQ'd. Exhaustion test: make it fail permanently; assert after max retries it lands in the DLQ and the offset advances. Chaos test: 100 good + 5 poison events; assert all 100 good processed, 5 in DLQ, no head-of-line blocking.
what_breaks_naive_approach: The poison event throws, the offset is never committed, Kafka redelivers the identical message forever, and every valid event behind it on the partition is blocked - the 40-minute, 1.2M-user notification outage.

title: Convert event-sourced account projection to atomically apply events with versioned snapshots
business_context: A fintech ledger is event-sourced: account events append to an event store and a read-model projection is updated. The projector applied events without tracking the last applied event version, so a projector restart re-applied already-projected events, double-counting balances in the read model. A deploy caused 7,200 accounts to show inflated balances for 25 minutes.
constraints: Projection must apply each event exactly once even across projector restarts
Events are strictly ordered per account by sequence number
Projection and applied-position must be updated atomically
Projector runs as a single writer per account partition but may restart any time
Rebuilds from scratch must be supported (snapshot + replay)
Read model is Postgres

before_state.description: The projector reads new events and applies deltas to the read model without recording the last applied sequence, so a restart reprocesses events.

issues: No applied-position tracking; a restart re-applies all events, double-counting
Read of events and write of balance are not coupled to a position update
No snapshot/rebuild support

reasoning: Naive fix: keep an in-memory cursor of the last event id. Rejected because the cursor is lost on restart, so reprocessing resumes from the start. Alternative: make each projection write idempotent by storing per-event applied flags. Rejected as too heavy (a flag per event per account) and still needs a position for efficient resume. Chosen path: store the last applied sequence per account in the same row as the balance, and apply each event in a transaction that bumps amount AND last_seq together, with a guard that only applies events whose seq == last_seq+1 (strict order, exactly once). On startup the projector reads each account's last_seq and resumes from there. Snapshots support rebuild. Cross-layer: event store -> projector reads from per-account last_seq -> tx applies delta + bumps last_seq with seq guard -> restart resumes from stored position -> snapshot for rebuild -> config none -> tests assert no double-apply on restart.

after_state.description: The projection row carries last_seq; each event applies in a transaction that bumps balance and last_seq only when seq == last_seq+1, making restarts and replays idempotent.

files.purpose: Per-account applied position for exactly-once projection and snapshots for fast rebuilds
Applies events in strict order with an atomic delta+position update; duplicates and restarts are no-ops
Deterministic rebuild from a snapshot plus replay, producing the same projection every time

migration_strategy: Add last_seq column; backfill it by replaying the event store once to set each account's correct position
Deploy the resume-from-position projector behind PROJ_V2=on in shadow comparing balances
Verify shadow projection matches the live one to the cent
Cut over to PROJ_V2 as the live projection; keep the old projector dark
Add snapshotting on a schedule and verify rebuild reproduces balances exactly
Remove the read-from-beginning projector after a clean deploy cycle with no double-counts
testing_strategy: Fixture appends a known event sequence (credits/debits) for several accounts. Apply, then restart the projector (re-invoke project) and assert balances are unchanged (no double-apply). Side-effect assert: each account's last_seq equals its max event seq. Out-of-order test: feed an event with seq gap; assert it is not applied (guard) and the projector stops to re-read. Rebuild test: snapshot at seq N, append more, rebuild from scratch; assert the rebuilt balance equals the live balance. Chaos test: kill the projector mid-sequence; resume and assert exactly-once application and correct final balance.
what_breaks_naive_approach: On a deploy/restart the projector re-reads the full event stream from the beginning with no applied position, re-applying every credit/debit and doubling balances - the 7,200 inflated accounts.

title: Stop lost loyalty-point grants by moving the grant inside the purchase transaction
business_context: A retail app grants loyalty points after a purchase via a separate async call. When the points service was slow, the call timed out and the grant was dropped, but the purchase committed - customers didn't get points for purchases they made. 22,000 missing grants triggered a class of complaints and a manual reconciliation that took the data team 2 days.
constraints: Every committed purchase must grant points exactly once
Points service may be temporarily unavailable
Grant must be idempotent per purchase (no double points on retry)
Purchase write path must stay under 60ms p95
Purchase and points live in different services (separate DBs)
Backward compatible points API

before_state.description: Purchase commits, then a fire-and-forget HTTP call grants points; a timeout drops the grant.

issues: Fire-and-forget grant: a timeout or points-service downtime drops the grant
No durability/retry; the grant is lost if the call fails
No idempotency; a retried grant double-credits points

reasoning: Naive fix: await the grant and retry inline. Rejected because it couples purchase latency to the points service and a crash mid-retry still drops the grant. Alternative: a nightly batch that grants points for ungranted purchases. Rejected because customers wait up to a day for points and the batch itself can fail. Chosen path: write a 'points.granted' outbox row atomically with the purchase; a relay delivers it to the points service with an idempotency key (the purchase id), and the points service dedupes on it. This keeps the purchase path fast and guarantees the grant is durable and exactly-once. Cross-layer: purchase -> INSERT purchase + outbox (one tx) -> relay -> points.grant(idempotencyKey=purchaseId) -> points dedupes -> config sets relay interval -> tests assert grant survives points downtime.

after_state.description: The grant intent is written to an outbox atomically with the purchase; a relay delivers it idempotently, so grants survive points-service downtime and never double-credit.

files.purpose: Writes the purchase and the points-grant intent atomically so a grant can never be silently dropped
Delivers grants with the purchase id as idempotency key; only marks published after success
Points service dedupes on the purchase-scoped key so a redelivered grant never double-credits

migration_strategy: Add the point_grants unique-key table in the points service; deploy idempotent grant endpoint
Switch purchase to write the outbox row (keep fire-and-forget dark) behind POINTS_OUTBOX=on
Enable the relay on a canary; verify grants apply exactly once
Replay the 22,000 historically missing grants through the relay with purchase-id keys
Ramp to 100%; remove the fire-and-forget call
Add an alert on outbox 'points.granted' rows unpublished > 5 min, then drop the flag
testing_strategy: Two-service fixture. Make a purchase; run the relay; assert the points service credited the user once. Side-effect assert: outbox row published, one point_grants row. Downtime chaos test: take the points service offline for 10s during purchases; assert the outbox rows persist and drain on recovery - zero lost grants. Idempotency test: re-run the relay over re-marked-unpublished rows; assert ON CONFLICT makes them no-ops and balances unchanged. Negative test: a rolled-back purchase writes no outbox row, so no grant is delivered.
what_breaks_naive_approach: When the points service is slow, the fire-and-forget grant times out and is swallowed while the purchase commits; the customer is permanently short their points with no retry - the 22,000 missing grants.

title: Fix non-idempotent PUT that creates duplicates instead of upserting in a CRUD API
business_context: A B2B integration platform exposes PUT /resources/:id for partners to sync records. The handler always INSERTs, so a partner's retry (after a 504 from a slow downstream) created duplicate resources with different surrogate keys. 3,100 duplicate records corrupted a partner's data export and broke their downstream pipeline.
constraints: PUT must be idempotent: same id + body yields one resource, repeated PUTs are no-ops
Partners retry aggressively on 5xx/timeouts
Concurrent PUTs for the same id must not create two rows
Must preserve created_at on update, only touch updated_at
Runs on 5 API replicas; Postgres backend
Response must distinguish created (201) from updated (200)

before_state.description: PUT always inserts a new row with a generated surrogate key, ignoring the path id, so retries duplicate.

issues: PUT always INSERTs; a retry creates a duplicate row for the same external_id
Ignores the path id as the identity; uses a fresh surrogate each time
Concurrent PUTs for the same id both insert

reasoning: Naive fix: SELECT by external_id then INSERT or UPDATE. Rejected because concurrent PUTs both SELECT 'not found' and both INSERT (TOCTOU). Alternative: application-level lock per id. Rejected because 5 replicas don't share it. Chosen path: a UNIQUE constraint on external_id and an INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (external_id) DO UPDATE upsert that is atomic and race-safe, returning whether the row was inserted or updated (via xmax/RETURNING trick) to choose 201 vs 200, and preserving created_at. Cross-layer: PUT -> upsert ON CONFLICT keyed by external_id -> created vs updated detection -> 201/200 -> concurrent retries collapse to one row -> migration adds unique + cleans dups -> tests assert idempotency under concurrent retries.

after_state.description: A unique external_id plus an atomic upsert makes PUT idempotent and race-safe, distinguishing created from updated and preserving created_at.

files.purpose: Unique external_id is the idempotency key; pre-clean enables the constraint
Atomic upsert keyed by external_id; xmax=0 detects insert vs update for correct 201/200 and preserves created_at

migration_strategy: Run the dedup query in a maintenance window; verify no external_id has two rows
Add the unique constraint
Deploy the upsert handler behind PUT_UPSERT=on for a canary partner
Verify retried PUTs return 200 and create no duplicates
Ramp to all partners; remove the insert-only handler
Add a monitor on any unique-violation attempt (should be zero post-rollout), then drop the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture with real Postgres. Fire 6 concurrent PUTs with the same path id and body across 2 app instances. Assert output: exactly one is 201, the rest 200. Side-effect assert: exactly one resources row, created_at unchanged across updates, updated_at advances. Idempotency test: PUT the same body twice; assert the row is byte-identical and no duplicate. Different-body test: PUT updated body; assert data changes, still one row. Migration test: seed duplicate external_ids, run dedup, assert the unique constraint builds and the earliest row survives.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A partner's retry after a 504 re-sends the same PUT; the insert-only handler generates a fresh surrogate and creates a duplicate for the same external_id - the 3,100 duplicates that corrupted the export.

title: Implement exactly-once Kafka-to-Kafka transform using transactional producer + read-committed
business_context: A data platform consumes raw clickstream from one Kafka topic, enriches it, and produces to an enriched topic. The pipeline used auto-commit and a plain producer, so crashes between produce and commit duplicated enriched records downstream. A consumer of the enriched topic (billing aggregation) over-billed advertisers by 6% one month before the duplicates were found.
constraints: Consume-transform-produce must be exactly-once end-to-end within Kafka
Throughput 60k records/sec; transactional overhead must stay acceptable
Downstream consumers must read only committed records (read_committed)
Pipeline runs 12 instances; transactional ids must be stable per partition for fencing
Rebalances must not duplicate or drop records
Backward compatible enriched topic schema

before_state.description: Auto-commit consumer + non-transactional producer. A crash between produce and offset commit reprocesses and re-produces, duplicating enriched records.

issues: Produce and offset commit are not atomic; a crash duplicates enriched records
Non-transactional producer; downstream sees uncommitted/duplicate records
No transactional id; rebalances and zombie instances can double-produce

reasoning: Naive fix: commit the offset before producing. Rejected because a crash after commit but before produce drops the record (at-most-once). Alternative: dedupe downstream by a record key. Rejected because it pushes correctness to every consumer and the enriched stream has no natural idempotency key for billing aggregation. Chosen path: Kafka's exactly-once semantics for consume-transform-produce - a transactional producer with a stable transactional.id per consumer-partition, sending the produced records AND the consumed offsets within one Kafka transaction (sendOffsetsToTransaction), and downstream consumers set isolation.level=read_committed. Zombie instances are fenced by the transactional id. Cross-layer: consume -> begin txn -> produce enriched + send offsets in txn -> commit txn atomically -> downstream read_committed -> config sets transactional.id + processing.guarantee -> tests assert no duplicates on crash.

after_state.description: A transactional producer commits the enriched records and the consumed offsets in one Kafka transaction; downstream reads committed-only; zombie fencing prevents double-produce.

files.purpose: Produces enriched records and commits consumed offsets in one Kafka transaction for exactly-once
Downstream reads committed-only so aborted/duplicate transactional batches are never billed
Pins EOS config: transactional timeout, in-flight limit, and read_committed isolation

migration_strategy: Deploy the enriched-topic downstream consumers with read_committed first (safe with current producer)
Roll the transactional producer with stable per-partition transactional.ids behind EOS=on for one partition set
Verify no duplicates downstream during induced crashes in staging
Ramp EOS to all instances; monitor transaction abort rate and end-to-end latency
Backfill/dedupe the historically over-billed window in the billing aggregation
Remove the auto-commit non-transactional path after a clean billing cycle
testing_strategy: Embedded Kafka with transaction support. Fixture publishes 10,000 raw records; run the pipeline; assert the enriched topic has exactly 10,000 records read under read_committed. Crash chaos test: kill the pipeline after a produce but before commit; restart; assert the aborted transaction's records are invisible to read_committed consumers and the batch is re-produced exactly once. Fencing test: start a zombie instance with the same transactional.id; assert it is fenced and cannot double-produce. Side-effect assert: a read_uncommitted observer sees aborted records but the read_committed billing consumer counts exactly 10,000. Negative test: an enrich() exception aborts the transaction, leaving no enriched records and uncommitted offsets.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A crash between producer.send and the separate auto-commit re-processes the batch on restart and re-produces the enriched records; downstream billing reads both copies and over-bills - the 6% over-billing.

title: Replace last-write-wins with CRDT-style merge for offline-first note sync
business_context: A note-taking app syncs notes across devices. The server used last-write-wins on a single body field, so when two devices edited offline and synced, one device's edits were silently overwritten. Power users lost paragraphs of notes; a viral tweet about 'losing my thesis notes' drove a churn spike and a refund wave.
constraints: Two devices can edit the same note offline and sync later; neither edit may be silently lost
Merge must be deterministic and converge regardless of sync order
Server stores authoritative state; clients sync deltas
No requirement for character-perfect OT, but no whole-field clobber
Sync runs at 50k notes/min; merge must be efficient
Backward compatible with existing note read API

before_state.description: Sync overwrites the server note body with whichever update arrives last, discarding the other device's changes.

issues: Last-write-wins overwrites the whole body; concurrent edits from another device are lost
Relies on wall-clock timestamps which skew across devices
No merge; divergent edits never reconcile

reasoning: Naive fix: reject the sync if the server changed since the client's base and force a manual conflict resolution. Rejected because forcing users to manually merge every concurrent edit is terrible UX for a notes app and most edits are non-conflicting. Alternative: full operational transformation (OT). Rejected because OT requires a central authoritative transform server and is heavy to implement correctly for this use case. Chosen path: model the note as a list of blocks with a Last-Writer-Wins-per-block plus an add/remove set CRDT (a simplified OR-Set of blocks with per-block Lamport timestamps), so concurrent edits to different blocks both survive and edits to the same block resolve deterministically by Lamport clock + replica id. Cross-layer: client computes block-level delta with Lamport clock -> sync merges into the server CRDT state -> deterministic convergence -> read API materializes merged body -> config none -> tests assert no lost edits and convergence regardless of order.

after_state.description: Notes are stored as a block CRDT (OR-Set with Lamport-stamped blocks). Sync merges deltas so concurrent edits to different blocks both survive and same-block edits resolve deterministically.

files.purpose: Block-level CRDT merge that is commutative, associative, idempotent so concurrent edits converge without loss
Merges the client delta into server CRDT state under a row lock and materializes the readable body

files.content:
ALTER TABLE notes ADD COLUMN state JSONB NOT NULL DEFAULT '{"blocks":{}}'::jsonb;
-- body kept as materialized view of state for backward-compatible reads
files.purpose: Stores CRDT state alongside the materialized body so existing readers are unaffected

migration_strategy: Add the state column; backfill by converting each existing body into a single-block CRDT
Ship clients that compute block-level deltas with Lamport clocks (dual-send old body + new delta)
Enable CRDT merge on sync behind CRDT_SYNC=on; keep materializing body for old readers
Verify on a cohort that concurrent multi-device edits no longer lose content
Drop the legacy whole-body sync path once clients are fully migrated
Remove dual-send and the flag after a clean two weeks with zero lost-edit reports
testing_strategy: Property-style fixture: generate two divergent edit sets (device A edits block 1, device B edits block 2 and block 1). Apply syncs in both orders. Assert convergence: merge(A,B) materialized == merge(B,A) materialized (order independence). Assert no-loss: block 2's edit survives even though A never touched it, and block 1 resolves to the higher Lamport edit deterministically. Idempotency test: apply the same delta twice; assert state unchanged. Chaos test: interleave 100 random deltas across 3 replicas in random order; assert all replicas converge to identical state. Negative test: an old whole-body sync is converted to a single-block delta and merged without clobbering other blocks.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Two devices edit offline; on sync the later wall-clock timestamp wins and overwrites the entire body, discarding the other device's paragraphs - the lost-thesis-notes incident.

title: Guarantee idempotent message handling with an inbox pattern in a NestJS consumer
business_context: A B2B SaaS consumes 'invoice.issued' events from RabbitMQ to create accounting entries. The consumer acks after processing, but on a transient DB error it nacks and the broker redelivers, re-creating the accounting entry. 1,500 duplicate ledger entries broke a customer's month-end close and required an accounting reversal.
constraints: Each message must be processed exactly once despite broker redelivery
Processing involves a non-idempotent ledger insert
Consumer runs 6 instances; the same message can be redelivered to a different instance
DB and message handling must be coupled so an ack reflects a committed effect
Transient errors must nack-and-retry without duplicating
RabbitMQ at-least-once delivery is fixed

before_state.description: The consumer processes and acks; on a transient error it nacks, and the redelivered message re-creates the ledger entry.

issues: No dedupe on message id; a redelivered message re-creates the ledger entry
Processing and ack are not atomic with a dedupe record
Transient nack causes duplicate on redelivery

reasoning: Naive fix: check if a ledger entry for the invoice exists before creating. Rejected because two redeliveries to different instances both read 'no entry' (TOCTOU). Alternative: rely on the broker's deduplication. Rejected because RabbitMQ doesn't dedupe redeliveries from a nack. Chosen path: the inbox pattern - record the message id in an inbox table with UNIQUE constraint inside the SAME transaction that creates the ledger entry; the unique insert is the atomic dedupe. A redelivery hits the unique violation, the handler treats it as already-processed and acks. Transient errors before commit nack safely because nothing committed. Cross-layer: consume -> begin tx -> insert inbox(message_id) + create ledger entry -> commit -> ack -> redelivery hits unique violation -> ack as duplicate -> config none -> tests assert single entry under redelivery.

after_state.description: The message id is recorded in an inbox table with a unique constraint in the same transaction as the ledger entry; redeliveries hit the unique violation and ack as duplicates.

files.purpose: Inbox ledger; PRIMARY KEY on message_id is the exactly-once dedupe guarantee
Atomic inbox insert + ledger entry; unique-violation = duplicate (ack), transient = safe requeue
Ledger insert runs inside the caller's transaction so it commits atomically with the inbox dedupe row

migration_strategy: Ensure the publisher sets a stable messageId per logical invoice event
Add the message_inbox table; deploy the inbox-guarded consumer behind INBOX=on for one queue
Replay a recorded redelivery storm in staging; verify single ledger entry
Ramp to all 6 instances; monitor the duplicate-ack counter as a redelivery signal
Add a sweeper pruning inbox rows older than the broker's max redelivery window
Remove the unguarded consumer after a clean month-end close
testing_strategy: Fixture with real Postgres and a fake RMQ channel. Deliver the same messageId 4 times concurrently across 2 instances. Assert output: one delivery commits, three ack as duplicates. Side-effect assert: exactly one ledger_entries row and one message_inbox row. Transient test: make createEntry fail with a transient error; assert the handler nacks (requeue), nothing is committed (no inbox row), and the redelivery succeeds creating exactly one entry. Chaos test: kill the instance between commit and ack; on redelivery the inbox unique violation acks the duplicate so no second entry. Negative test: a message with no messageId is rejected to a DLQ rather than risking an un-deduplicatable insert.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A transient DB error nacks the message; the broker redelivers it (possibly to another instance) and the non-idempotent createEntry runs again, duplicating the ledger entry - the 1,500 duplicates that broke month-end close.

title: Coordinate a multi-step KYC onboarding saga with timeouts and human-in-the-loop steps
business_context: A fintech onboards users through a saga: create profile -> run KYC provider check -> provision account -> if KYC flags, route to manual review. The old linear code blocked on the KYC provider and, when manual review was needed, lost the saga context, so 380 users were stuck half-onboarded with funds they couldn't access, drawing regulator attention.
constraints: Saga includes an asynchronous human-review step that may take hours/days
Each automated step must be idempotent and resumable after restart
A KYC provider timeout must not fail the whole onboarding; it must retry or escalate
Manual review approval/rejection events must resume the saga at the right step
Saga state persisted; workers are 4 replicas
Compensation must revoke a provisioned account if KYC ultimately fails

before_state.description: A linear function blocks on KYC and provisioning; the manual-review branch has no persistence, so context is lost when review takes time.

issues: Blocks on KYC; a long manual review loses saga context
No persistence; a restart during review leaves the user half-onboarded
No compensation if review later rejects an already-provisioned account

reasoning: Naive fix: poll the KYC provider in a loop until it resolves. Rejected because manual review can take days and a polling loop dies with the process and blocks a worker. Alternative: a fully synchronous state machine in memory. Rejected because in-memory state can't survive the multi-day human step. Chosen path: a persisted saga state machine where the manual-review step parks the saga in a 'awaiting_review' state; an external review-decision event (webhook) resumes it. Each step is idempotent and the saga records current_state in Postgres. A timeout sweeper escalates KYC provider timeouts. If review rejects after provisioning, a compensation revokes the account. Cross-layer: API -> persist saga -> idempotent createProfile -> KYC check with timeout -> pass->provision / review->park -> review webhook resumes -> reject->compensate revoke -> sweeper escalates timeouts -> config sets deadlines -> tests assert resumable across the human step.

after_state.description: A persisted saga state machine parks at manual review and resumes on the review-decision webhook; steps are idempotent and a rejection after provisioning triggers account revocation.

files.purpose: Durable saga state; parks at awaiting_review and tracks provisioning for compensation
Persists each transition; on KYC timeout it parks for the sweeper; review parks for the webhook
Resumes the parked saga on the human decision; compensates by revoking the account on rejection
Escalates KYC timeouts by reconciling provider status or routing to manual review, so nobody is stuck

migration_strategy: Add the onboarding_sagas table; new onboardings persist state behind ONBOARD_SAGA=on
Make createProfile/provisionAccount/revokeAccount idempotent (keyed by user_id)
Wire the review-decision webhook to resume parked sagas
Enable the KYC timeout sweeper on a single leader; verify escalation in staging
Migrate the 380 stuck users into the saga table and let the sweeper/webhook resolve them
Ramp to 100%; remove the linear blocking onboard after a clean compliance review
testing_strategy: Fixture with a fake KYC provider configurable to pass/review/reject/timeout. Pass path: onboard -> assert state 'active', account provisioned once. Review path: onboard -> assert state 'awaiting_review', no account; then deliver approve webhook -> assert provisioned and active. Reject-after-provision test: approve provisions, then a later reject decision -> assert revokeAccount called and state 'rejected'. Timeout chaos test: KYC call times out; run sweeper -> assert it reconciles via status or routes to review (user not stuck). Idempotency test: deliver the same review decision twice; assert one provisioning, no double action. Restart test: kill the worker mid-onboard; assert the saga resumes from persisted state.
what_breaks_naive_approach: When KYC returns 'review', the fire-and-forget routeToManualReview loses the saga context and the blocking call returns; a restart during the multi-day review leaves the user half-onboarded with inaccessible funds - the 380 stuck users.

title: Fix MongoDB counter drift by replacing read-then-write with atomic $inc and findOneAndUpdate
business_context: A social app tracks per-post like counts in MongoDB. The like handler reads the count, increments in app, and saves; concurrent likes lost updates and the displayed count drifted below reality. A celebrity post showed 1.2M likes when the like documents totaled 1.47M - a 270k discrepancy that became a PR embarrassment.
constraints: Up to 5,000 concurrent likes/sec on a viral post
The counter must equal the true number of like documents
Likes are idempotent per (user, post) - a user can like once
MongoDB replica set; no multi-doc transaction needed for the hot counter path
Counter reads for display can be eventually consistent
Backward compatible posts schema

before_state.description: Like reads the post, increments likeCount in app, and saves the whole document; concurrent likes clobber each other.

issues: Read-modify-write of likeCount loses concurrent increments
replaceOne overwrites other concurrent field updates too
likes collection has no unique index; a user can like twice, inflating the count

reasoning: Naive fix: use findOneAndUpdate with $inc. Rejected as incomplete because $inc fixes the counter race but without a unique like document a user can still like multiple times and inflate the count, and the count can drift from the true like-doc total. Alternative: recompute the count from likes on every read. Rejected because counting 1.47M docs per read is too slow at 5k/sec. Chosen path: a unique compound index on (postId, userId) makes likes idempotent; the like handler first inserts the like document (unique) and only $inc the counter when the insert actually created a new like - this couples the counter to the true like set, and $inc is atomic so concurrent likes don't lose updates. A periodic reconciler corrects any drift. Cross-layer: like -> unique insert like doc -> on new insert, atomic $inc counter -> duplicate like is a no-op -> reconciler fixes drift -> config none -> tests assert count == like-doc total under concurrency.

after_state.description: A unique (postId,userId) like index makes likes idempotent; the counter is $inc only when a new like is created, coupling it to the true like-doc total, with a reconciler for drift.

files.content: db.likes.createIndex({ postId: 1, userId: 1 }, { unique: true });
files.purpose: Idempotent likes; a duplicate like throws 11000 instead of inflating the count
Couples the atomic counter to actual like-doc creation/removal so the count can't drift from reality
Periodic reconciliation corrects any residual drift to the authoritative like-document count

migration_strategy: Create the unique (postId,userId) index after de-duplicating existing like docs
Deploy the insert-gated $inc like handler behind LIKE_ATOMIC=on for a canary set of posts
Run reconcilePost over all posts once to correct historical drift
Verify the canary posts' counts match like-doc totals under load
Ramp to 100%; remove the read-modify-write path
Schedule periodic reconciliation for top posts and drop the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture with a real replica set. Fire 5,000 concurrent likes from distinct users on one post. Assert output: likeCount equals 5,000. Side-effect assert: likeCount equals countDocuments(likes) for the post (no drift). Idempotency test: 1,000 concurrent duplicate likes from the same user; assert one like doc and the count increments by exactly 1. Unlike test: like then unlike concurrently; assert count returns to baseline and stays coupled to like docs. Drift test: artificially corrupt likeCount, run reconcile; assert it restores the true count. Chaos test: kill the process between the like insert and the $inc; assert the reconciler later corrects the off-by-one.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Concurrent likes all read likeCount=N, each compute N+1, and replaceOne overwrites the others - most increments are lost, so the displayed count falls far below the true like-doc total, the 270k discrepancy.
